### ATL_Paleo — 2013-09-02T02:56:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aussies-and-the-donut-hole.8115/post-94630>

Dr. K .... here are a few tidbits that are probably related to the answer you are looking for .....

Is there a hole over the Arctic? .... Unlike Antarctica, which is a continent surrounded by oceans, the Arctic is an ocean surrounded by mountainous continents. This means that the stratospheric circulation is much more irregular. Because it is not as cold as the Antarctic, stratospheric clouds are less common. So a deep ozone hole over the North Pole is unlikely, but limited ozone depletion can occur above parts of the Arctic, though it lasts only a few days at a time. [http://www.theozonehole.com/basscience.htm](http://www.theozonehole.com/basscience.htm)

The ozone donut hole reaches its maximum size around the fall equinox. My intuition tells me that the graphs on this page are important to your question. [http://www.theozonehole.com/2012ozonehole.htm](http://www.theozonehole.com/2012ozonehole.htm)

Polar night terminator: the delimiter between the polar night (continual darkness during winter over the Antarctic) and the encroaching sunlight. By the first week of October the polar night has ended at the South Pole. [http://www.environment.gov.au/atmosphere/ozone/publications/ozone-reports.html](http://www.environment.gov.au/atmosphere/ozone/publications/ozone-reports.html)

### Jack Kruse — 2013-09-02T03:22:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aussies-and-the-donut-hole.8115/post-94633>

Part of the answer is on one of the graphs. [http://phys.org/news/2013-06-climate-war-disrupting-australian-atmospheric.html](http://phys.org/news/2013-06-climate-war-disrupting-australian-atmospheric.html)

### Jack Kruse — 2013-09-02T03:55:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aussies-and-the-donut-hole.8115/post-94636>

The sun's rays provide both light and heat to Earth, and regions that receive greater exposure warm to a greater extent. This is particularly true of the tropics, which experience less seasonal variation in incident sunlight. Moisture-laden tropical air warms, becomes less dense, and rises. But as air reaches the upper levels of the atmosphere, it cools. Water molecules condense to form clouds and eventually fall as rain. Warm air rising from Earth's surface pushes the air mass away from the equator, and releases its moisture as precipitation as it travels pole-ward

If the Earth did not spin on its axis, this cycle of evaporation, condensation, and precipitation would move water and air along a north-south axis from the equator to the poles. This, however, does not happen. Earth's spin creates three belts of circulation (Figure 2). Air circulates from the tropics to regions approximately 30Â° north and south latitude, where the air masses sink. This belt of air circulation is referred to as a Hadley cell, after George Hadley, who first described it (Holton 2004). Two additional belts of circulating air exist in the temperate latitudes (between 30Â° and 60Â° latitude) and near the poles (between 60Â° and 90Â° latitude).

The sinking air mass at 30˚ latitude drives two phenomena: It contributes to the formation of arid climates and drives circulation of air north and south of the tropics. Dry, even desert-like conditions often occur at 30˚ north and south latitude because the descending dry air draws moisture from the soil. As warm air rises in the tropics, cool air is drawn from surrounding areas to fill the void. This creates the trade winds that blow in subtropical regions. But some of the air that descends from the Hadley cell is drawn away from the equator toward the poles. This air mass creates winds that characterize weather patterns in the temperate zones.

Earth's rotation affects the oceans in a similar manner, setting up currents that flow within the ocean basins. Ocean currents are driven by surface winds, Earth's rotation, and differences in salinity.

Trade winds blow warm surface waters in tropical oceans and seas from east to west. Warm water pools along the west coast of continents, which sets up a temperature gradient across the ocean surface. Under normal conditions, the western Pacific is about 8Â°C warmer than the eastern Pacific, and this gradient contributes to the **formation of clouds and precipitation in Australia**, Indonesia, and parts of Africa. Disruption of this temperature gradient creates the event known as El NiÃ±o.

Movement of water away from the coast of Peru and Ecuador creates an upwelling, as cold water is drawn from below to fill the space. Similar conditions occur on the west coast of continents in the Atlantic and Indian Oceans. These regions are the primary source of mixing between **warmer surface waters** and colder deep waters, which ordinarily remain separate on the basis of water density tied to temperature differential. The upwelling of nutrient-rich water contributes to the unusually high biological productivity of the coastal waters in these regions.

Just as Earth's rotation creates the prevailing winds, it creates surface currents within the oceans. Under the influence of the trade winds, surface waters near the equator flow from east to west. As in the atmosphere, the Coriolis force causes water to be deflected away from the equator (northward in the Northern Hemisphere, southward in the Southern Hemisphere). This Coriolis Effect sets up a rotational convection within the oceans, and currents typically flow in a clockwise rotation in the Northern Hemisphere and in a counter-clockwise direction in the Southern Hemisphere. As it reaches the poles, the water cools and sinks. Prevailing winds in northern and southern latitudes help to create cold-water surface currents that flow back toward the equator along the west coast of continents.

Surface waters freeze as they reach the arctic waters of the North Atlantic. The freezing process removes water molecules, but not salt, from the ocean. The result is an increase in the salinity of ocean waters. With increased salinity and decreased temperature comes greater density â€” water is densest at 4Â°C â€” and the water sinks to the ocean floor. This process sets up a large, slow, deep-water "conveyor belt" that transports water along the ocean floor to Antarctica then through the Indian, Pacific, and eventually Atlantic oceans.

Global Climate
The combination of oceanic and atmospheric circulation drives global climate by redistributing heat and moisture. Areas located near the tropics remain warm and relatively wet throughout the year. In temperate regions, variation in solar input drives seasonal changes. In the Northern Hemisphere where land masses are more concentrated, these seasons can involve pronounced changes in temperature. In the Southern Hemisphere where large land masses are located nearer to the equator and the majority of Earth's surface is covered with water, seasonal cycles revolve around the presence and absence of precipitation rather than major swings in temperature.

Global climate patterns are dynamic: They are continually changing in response to solar radiation, atmospheric greenhouse gas concentrations, and other climate forcing factors. Among the more predictable of these changes are cyclical changes in solar radiation reaching the poles. These cycles, first described by Milutin Milankovitch (1941), involve Earth's orbit, tilt, and the precession of the equinoxes. You may remember that I wrote about these cycles in the last chapter of my book. **It was a critical piece of information on figuring out what constituted the Epi-paleo Rx.**

Earth's elliptical orbit around the sun shifts under the gravitational pull of other planets in our solar system. In a 100,000-year cycle, the orbit shifts from one that is nearly circular to one that is elongated, pulling the planet further from its energy source, the sun! Earth's tilt relative to its orbit changes in a 41,000-year cycle from 21.5Â° to 24.5Â°; **we are currently in the middle of this cycle with a tilt of 23.5**Â°. Finally, the axis (north-south orientation) of the Earth wobbles over time. This 23,000-year precession of the equinoxes changes the orientation of the planet relative to its location in orbit. **When all three Milankovitch cycles reinforce each other, they alter solar input and influence oceanic and atmospheric circulation patterns.** This can lead to regular periods of cooling and glaciation.

**Milankovitch cycles**
(A) High eccentricity in Earth's orbit takes it further away from the sun. (B) The degree of Earth's tilt relative to its plane of orbit changes the degree of warming in the polar regions. (C) Precession of the equinoxes occurs as Earth wobbles on its axis. All three cycles can influence warming and cooling periods by **altering the amount of solar radiation that reaches Earth.**
The sun's incident angle drives the atmospheric currents via's it photoelectric properties..........but the donut hole has two other effects on the Southern Ocean.

The same logic described in the '1918 Flu Pandemic' section of the recent 'Energy and Epigenetics 3' (EE3) post then follows.

I noticed that the 1918 Flu hit hardest during the Summer to early Autumn of 1920, and then rapidly declined to almost nothing in early 1921. Is there a corresponding change in magnetism that could be at root here? Or is this just the usual case that the virus mutated to a less harmful variety.

\---

The Sun's magnetic field is supposedly set to flip again in the coming months, as part its regular 11-year cycle. I'll definitely be looking out to see how the magnetic strength of the field where I'm at (Australia) ends up faring. But as Dr Kruse mentioned in the EE3 post, the outlook is still pretty flaky.

\---

My ancestry actually stems from South China, but I myself was born in Melbourne Australia.

Referencing the 'Does Where you Live Matter?' post, the part of China were my ancestors came from has very similar temperature and proximity to the ocean as where I am now, with what I think is slightly lower barometric pressure, similar average elevation (20+m in northern guangzhou vs 30+m in Melbourne). Obviously the longitudes vary wildly, though I'm only 2hrs apart latitude-wise from my place of ancestry.

I'm led to wonder how much of an effect that "mismatch" would have, but obviously haven't had the time to test it. Wouldn't relish the thought of running back to a polluted China right now (inundated by chemicals and EMF).

The fact that EMFs can escape through ozone may be a double edged sword - nature abhors a vacuum.

See [http://jackkruse.com/forum/showthread.php?8087-Quantum-tie-in-to-EMF](http://jackkruse.com/forum/showthread.php?8087-Quantum-tie-in-to-EMF)

key phrase

5\. The biggest effect on climate and water is the industrial use of ozone-depleting chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) (Southern Hemisphere alert enlarged donut hole) that **change the dielectric properties of water in our atmosphere**.

Earths natural magnetic field is decreasing due to possible pole reversal
Solar winds cause damage to magnetic field further reducing ozone layer due to loss of protection
Reduced ozone means high UV radiation
High UV radiation = increased cancer
Excessive UV-B inhibits the growth processes of almost all green plants
and insects and impacts this close inter relationship

Nasa reports that in the north Pacific ocean oxygen-producing phytoplankton concentrations are 30% lower today, compared to the 1980s. This is a huge drop in just three decades.

What are the effects of man-made EMFs on the macrocosm? This is where Jack is heading where microcosm and macrocosm meet.

So - when you were making big money and enjoying the good life - it was all about being ale to acquire stuff...no relevance to a real and honest way of being.....

I have to think about this more......But some things are exquisite - like a bottle of Chateaux Margaux or a fine brandy or a lovely meal with a loved one. But simplicity is wonderful too. It doesn't have to be expensive to be exquisite either...

If you have worked really hard all your life with honestly and integrity and you can afford these amazing pleasures .....just for the sheer joy of what this world has to offer......

I have tasted Chateau Margaux - and to be honest - I don't remember it really ...but we had a lot of amazing things that nite. One of my favorite memories is dinner at K Paul's in NOLA. I loved the cajun martinis and I got a star on my forehead! I guess you could say that it doesn't take much to make me happy - but I was with the one I loved....

### prAna303 — 2013-09-09T11:53:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aussies-and-the-donut-hole.8115/post-95334>

> So - when you were making big money and enjoying the good life - it was all about being ale to acquire stuff...no relevance to a real and honest way of being.....

Many times things goes overhead and end up in weird situations, not really the things i wanted so i got out of it in time. When you are in that flow you think most of the time that it is honest. Today many of my friends have gotten a taste of it and they are craving it big time... Same with technology, homes and other things like the obsession with our bodies. Just look at the amount of books about cooking, diets and enlightment, reminds me of the peak of porno in the end of 90Â´s.

> But some things are exquisite

You are right and these things we should treat with respect. Take that bottle of wine for example, made by nature and composed by human, a craftmanship, a dedication and passion. When we drink that fluid it is history that goes into us. When we live simple we give pure art, luxury and other things that the other around us calls snobbery the right attention. We also salutes the ones that create it.

Life is exquisite, not something that we should treat like dirt...

In this country we had a famous drug addict that nailed it:

"When someones is so deep down as i have been you stand with one foot in the past, the other in the future and you take a piss on the now"
\- Kenta

### yewwei.tan — 2013-09-10T12:51:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aussies-and-the-donut-hole.8115/post-95432>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=95417)
>
> all the fresh water falls on Oz from the atmosphere.......I just showed you in the blog where that water comes from.........You think any of that water is good after reading that blog? If you do......then you better re read it about 100 times. It should be crystal clear.

If I'm understanding the blog correctly, the major effect of the donut hole on water in Australia is that CFCs in the atmosphere get liberated as halide anions, and those ions end up in the rainwater that falls on Australia. This makes all rainwater essentially tainted by these dielectric blockers.

Wouldn't passing that water through a process that removes the halides counteract the negative effects of drinking that water?

If so, then at least having a good RO filter pump would allow for good water. As a temporary measure, I'm relying on water from Nobles Pureau, which does go through a RO process -- [http://www.noblebeverages.com/why-pureau/the-pureau-process/](http://www.noblebeverages.com/why-pureau/the-pureau-process/)

The potential negative effects of the donut definitely extend far beyond water for consumption, but at least we're getting one step closer to optimal . I still need to think about the potential measure for dealing with those direct gamma rays though ...

### Jack Kruse — 2013-09-10T15:02:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aussies-and-the-donut-hole.8115/post-95433>

There is really no good way to remove halides in water well......the best way is to desalinate sea water to RO water. It cuts all the issues of the water cycle in the donut hole out of it.........and as for NZ: they are not much better off because their water supplies come mostly from the same sources as Oz. Its best to get your water from somewhere else. I think in Oz of NZ the best bet today is import it.

you're missing the point........water falls on Oz and NZ from the sky where this bad quantum energy transfers are happening.......this means most of the water used by plants, food, and humans from the sky is no longer optimal.......but sea water does not come primarily from the sky and get replenished in the thermohaline currents. So when the water from the sea is turned into RO water......it should be used for crops, fruit, farming, and drinking.......in Oz and NZ.

Water is involved in all things living..........and that is why Oz has many of the issues it does.......all tied to the reaction of the donut hole by massive solar EMF that shortens waters hydrogen bonding angles in the atmosphere.......you know what it does in the atmosphere? It changes the atmospheric barometric pressures.

Atmospheric pressure affects the liquid water temperature range that is commonly used to define planetary habitability. if you wish to estimate habitability, you should explicitly take into account pressure in your problem. No one in paleo has a clue about water chemistry. On Earth, the space around us is filled with air molecules that collectively weigh on our bodies. Although you cannot feel it, Earth's atmosphere presses down with the force of one kilogram per square centimeter, or 14.7 pounds per square inch. That pressure works out to about a ton per square foot. Our terrestrial biology evolved to operate in this pressure, which, while startling-sounding, pales when compared to underwater creatures' bodies in the deep sea that can withstand dozens of tons per square foot. **Atmospheric pressure has an impact on water's boiling point when it transitions from a liquid to a gas.** As anyone who has cooked at high altitudes has experienced, water boils there at a lower temperature than the typical 100 degrees Celsius (212 degrees Fahrenheit). The reason: Atmospheric pressure is lower at high altitudes than at sea level; there is simply less atmosphere pressing down the higher you go up in the mountains. Lower pressure is why it takes a few minutes longer to make pasta in mile-high Denver than below-sea-level New Orleans—the pasta has to soak and soften in the water longer to become al dente in Denver because the water is boiling at a slightly lower temperature. It also has an effect on the freezing temperature of water too. Storms are associated with lower barometric pressures.......when this happens it changes the density of water everywhere........including in our body and our cells........in the podcast above I told you because of the atomic size of water 98% of molecules in our body is water because the big proteins and fats in us have large atomic sizes. Water dominates and remember it is a magnetic dipole. Here is where magnetism plays a role. The higher ratio of water molecules to protein means the responsiveness of the cell, organism, and it DNA is directly proportional to the electromagnetic field it is in. Remember water is a magnetic dipole. this is what electrifies DNA, proteins, and water itself. This is where energy is created in a cell. When a cell is dehydrated it is less electrified or magnified. It means that the expression of that protein or DNA is altered in direct proportion to the field water is in and the amount of water that is present.

### PaulG — 2013-09-11T12:46:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aussies-and-the-donut-hole.8115/post-95590>

Maybe not so bad for Perthites..

In the Perth Metropolitan area over 90% of rainfall runs off and is lost to sea. On average 5% of precipitation is harvested.
In addition due to first Australian desalination plant in Kwinana in 2006 about 50% of domestic water is from desalination intermixed with groundwater, surface water, reservoirs etc.
Ozone hole is shrinking [http://www.abc.net.au/science/articles/2012/10/25/3618542.htm](http://www.abc.net.au/science/articles/2012/10/25/3618542.htm)

So to be optimal options would be

1) Drink bottled European water at $2.00 for 1.5 litres - what is the structural effect on water being shipped one side of planet to t'other side?
2) Find a source of 100% desalinated water - hmm maybe a business opportunity - who remembers Only Fools and horses and Peckham Spring water?
3) Install RO mains unit to remove the fluoride and take the hit on 50% gamma radiation - maybe research whether melanoma cancer rates are higher when rainwater consumption is higher

What about water used in teas and coffees and used for cooking. I can't see myself pouring bottle of san pellegrino into the kettle

### yewwei.tan — 2013-09-11T13:29:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aussies-and-the-donut-hole.8115/post-95592>

> [Jude said:](/goto/post?id=95564)
>
> I can't stop laughing...sorry.....My dh reckons you should just keep hitting the beach and drink scotch!!
> The water problem. Well, for drinking perhaps we just may be able to side step that problem?......I'm pretty sure the water from the great Australian Artesian Basin is ok water-meaning no halides. It's millions of yrs old they say........what we have at present is not really drinkable..stock do well on it though.

I was hoping that we'd have good sources of groundwater, but some of the reports that I found indicated otherwise regarding the water from the Great Artesian Basin (GAB).

This paper seems to say that the GAB water can have fluoride content of up to 10mg/L (when the current high recommendation for water fluoridation is 1.0mg/L) -- [http://www.gabcc.org.au/tools/getFile.aspx?tbl=tblContentItem&id=42](http://www.gabcc.org.au/tools/getFile.aspx?tbl=tblContentItem&id=42)
This other paper also seems to point in the same direction -- [http://www.citeulike.org/user/wqunit/article/10157626](http://www.citeulike.org/user/wqunit/article/10157626)

Surface water can also get into the basin via recharge zones, though I doubt would have significantly affected the GAB halide concentrations given the recency of our donut hole problem.

\---

Jack is right that reverse osmosis (RO) doesn't remove all of the fluoride. It's pretty good though, ranging anywhere from 90-99% removal depending on the RO mechanism used.

@Jude: Regarding Pureau water, I haven't contacted them yet, but they claim that they have essentially zero levels of chloride and fluoride in the water (through a proprietary 3-step filtration process which includes RO), and meticulously test to ensure that the claim holds true. Nothing mentioned about bromide though.

For now, I don't mind the slight expense ($7.70 for 10L at woolies) while trying to reverse some nagging issues, but would probably look toward a RO filter installed in the house at some point.

\---

The above still doesn't address the incoherent water problem though ...

Hi Determined, Sorry about the delay, I had to reset my password to get back on the forum. To answer your question yes it will fit easily on a trailer, it comes in two halves in two boxes that are around 40x40x100cm and weigh around 20-30kg each. Pay attn to how how you unroll them as there is only one way that they can be rolled back up without damaging them, might be worth marking them with a Niko when you first receive them and before you unroll them. There is also a sticker that says which side up in the Southern Hemisphere.

For the 10G QS we paid a touch under AU$2k when the dollar was at $1.04, we also paid about $350 customs duty which I forgot totally about until I asked my wife tonight. I am totally happy with the value even though it is a steep initial price.

Hi Caroline,

I'm not craving more cold just yet but I have slept outside the past couple of weeks and I LOVE the cool that comes in the night!!
I'm ice-bathing 2 x per week ATM and though my kids stopped for a break in November (after a year of CT for them!) one of them is asking to get back into it so I'm going to adjust my timing so he can take advantage of the ice water after me. What's interesting to me is this son (my youngest) had some neuro-physical problems revealed late last year and, basically, I think he knows what's good for him!!

 Anne.

I apologize for using this system incorrectly but I cannot figure out how to initiate a post of my own. Please someone advise me or point me to the correct instructions. I'm generally pretty good at this stuff!

My question has to do with basal cell carcinoma. I'm a 39 year old woman who burned in the sun a ton as a kid. I haven't gone out in it much in the last 20 years except for the last few 20 minutes at a time. I have had 6 fairly extreme surgeries for confirmed basal cell carcinoma- on my face, back, clavicle and will be going in again for another on the top of my head. The scars are between one and 7 inches long. It's very extreme. They call Mohs surgery the "gold standard" and yet the scars are so extreme.

I am engaging in protocols- grounding, EFAs, Vit. D, nascent iodine internally, good sunlight, restructured water, organic food, prayer and meditation, plus essential oils directly on the spots. I live in the San Francisco Bay Area, I'm not sure what else to do. None of these things seem to be assisting my trajectory of a few surgeries per year.

Comments welcome and again, so sorry to not post in the right place. I've been feeling pretty desperate. Thank you,

Freya

### caroline — 2014-01-05T00:58:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aussies-and-the-donut-hole.8115/post-104745>

Hi Freya ... and welcome! no need for apologies what so ever! we are all in the same boat.

Please start a journal in the journal section. Go to the top of this page and click on forum and then go down six categories. The journal section is in the last one. Just start a new thread. I will watch for you!

Dr. Kruse likes Journals .... he needs context - so tell us more about yourself and your health history. Also just pop into the "meet and greet" section and say Hi -so we can properly welcome you!

Do you have labs you can post? There is a "post your Lab section". There are folks here who are great at reading labs.

Have you started the Leptin reset? using the epi paleo template? Please go to the home page and read the easy start guides - they are great.

Have you listened to Jack's podcasts? great for easy and fast info. There is also a great free podcast by Dr. Tim Jackson.

Do you have Dr. K's book? The Epi-Paleo RX ... available in kindle or paper - on Amazon. This will give you some insight into what Dr. K. is all about and what he is trying to accomplish.

May I suggest that you become a gold member if you can manage it. You will then have access to all of the webinars and the follow up live Q&A a few days later where you can ask Jack anything. They go for at least 2 hours and he has been know to talk for 4 hours!

Please keep reading and asking questions....if you don't know something you need to just ask!

Limiting non native EMF is huge for all of us....and C/T is a game changer!

### QuantumRadical — 2014-01-05T01:08:11+0200

The earth is now at perihelion--the closest it gets to the sun all year. So for those in the southern hemisphere, down under, you are being exposed to the hottest sun you are going to get being so close. And with that donut, brutal exposure. The rest of us get a bit of a respite because of the inclination of the northern hemisphere away from the sun. Enjoy the BOIL!!!

R

Caroline that's not good.
How much water are you drinking per day?
What time are you going to the beach? Perhaps you need to be back inside by 10?
Perhaps our age !, plus Bun/C levels, plus lower hormone panels makes us very susceptible to the heat/dehydration.

Meeting Nick made me more aware of water consumption..he is like JK and drinking tons..both are younger than us and in better shape. Was good over winter but as summer became so hectic I really didn't notice how my consumption dropped off and haven't been as careful with water consumption over the Christmas period and unfortunately I've paid for it!.

On New Yr's day had an emergency drive(took 9hrs) to an opthalmologist to diagnose a ? detached retina.....honestly am so disappointed. Here I am telling everyone how to get healthy and look at me! I'm lucky as the retina didn't detach, just bled reducing my vision. So was patted on the head,told not to bungy jump but live normally, and warned that it most probably will happen to my other eye. The specalist said the integrity of the vitreous humor had changed and does as we age..becomes more watery and then probs ensue......I was really scared ..have a friend who's lost an eye from this condition and another with very limited vision........

So, by my reckoning the change in integrity has been occurred because of dehydration, precipitated by a couple of days working in intense heat with insufficient hydration, no doubt coupled with all the other factors we know about. I'm drinking as I type! Perhaps there should be a thread for the over 60's..cause I think we would be much slower to return to optimal and haven't read younger folk post about heatstroke and thumping crazy hearts?

Caroline, out of curiosity, what is your particular CT protocol. Style (bath, shower, ice packs, etc.), temp range, length of time, frequency per week, per day,...etc.

Jack has talked a lot about doing a lot of CT. I have really been thinking about what he has said in the past, and I have come to the conclusion that 1 to 3 hours of CT a day is highly recommended, if not more.

I do it 2 or 3 times a week, water about 50 degrees in a large tub up to my neck, for about 30 - 45 minutes. I have done it more often and less often. If I don't do it for a few days, I believe my body begins to crave the cold. I have gradually been upping my CT and I have to admit, it works, but it is not linear--that is, some days it is easy, some days I dread the thought.

R

### PaulG — 2014-02-08T03:26:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aussies-and-the-donut-hole.8115/post-109004>

Here is my take for people living in Australia

From best to worse

1.  Bottled European water in glass where water has no to lowish natural fluoride and a ph between 7 and 8.5 eg. acqua panna
2.  Bottled European water in non bpa plastic. As water has to be shipped many miles it has spent many months in cargo holds and warehouses disconnected from earth, possibly in high temperatures in OZ warehouses
3.  Bottled Australian spring water - so long as water is derived from deep aquifers where water is hundreds of years old and not 'contaminated' with water derived from ozone hole weather system
4.  Bottled Reverse Osmosis from shops as it removes 98% of fluoride (Australian water is fluoridated)
5.  Reverse Osmosis water from home RO system, stages need replacing plus 6-12 months waste water is generated
6.  Tap water with good charcoal based filter

I was tempted to put down bottled european red wine as this is what the ancients drank but I am quite tee-total these days. Maybe grape juice!

So, personally we use 6 for cooking but am thinking of getting a [portable 6 stage RO unit](http://www.ebay.com.au/itm/6-STAGE-Portable-Reverse-Osmosis-ALKALIZING-Water-Filter-System-1-70SHUTTLE-/231119314896?pt=AU_SmallKitchenAppliances&hash=item35cfc8dfd0&_uhb=1) that includes an Infrared and magnetic stages but have been told by makers that ro filter only lasts 3-6 months in WA

We use 3. for drinking which works out at 0.88cents per litre but looking to bulk buy 2. which will work out 1.22 cents per litre maybe less. Still both very expensive compared to tap water. If WA government decide to drop fluoridation and [Dr. Paul Connett](http://www.meetup.com/Fluoride-Free-WA/events/153430862/) is addressing them this month in Perth I will re-assess use of filtered tap water but prefer to stay away if possible. Will look into acqua panna in glass which is 2.99 per litre from Dan Murpheys but I drink a lot of water whereas kids and wife don't despite my many protestations.

I also vortex my drinking water

Additionally I am looking to putting drinking water in glass and then chill in freezer for ten minutes and then stand in sunshine for ten minutes which should in theory maximize the EZicity around hydrophylic surface. Btw all of this is making me very ocd.

This is good link that compares bottled mineral water contents
[http://www.aquadeli.co.nz/santaVittoriaTraditional.php](http://www.aquadeli.co.nz/santaVittoriaTraditional.php)

### Jack Kruse — 2014-02-11T03:54:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aussies-and-the-donut-hole.8115/post-109553>

Randy said on Jan 4th, "The earth is now at perihelion--the closest it gets to the sun all year. So for those in the southern hemisphere, down under, you are being exposed to the hottest sun you are going to get being so close. And with that donut, brutal exposure. The rest of us get a bit of a respite because of the inclination of the northern hemisphere away from the sun. Enjoy the BOIL!!!"

Here is something you might not have considered. It shows you how the fourth phase of water is very altered in OZ and AUS and is very important for the donut hole dynamics............at the perihelion it has recently been shown radioactive decay rates alter tremendously compared to what it is on July 4th. This means that decay rates also have circadian seasons. This has huge implications for patients on radioactive drugs, who get radioactive tests, and when you get Xrays. The closer we are to the sun the more radiation we get........so the danger in the donut hole peaks at the Perihelion on Jan 4 every year because more of the sun's total electromagnetic spectrum comes toward Earth. It is less risky on July 4th because we are further away. In the Northern hemisphere we don't face this cyclic radiative barrage because the ozone layer block out the excess. Stratospheric ozone (sometimes referred to as "good ozone") plays a beneficial role by absorbing most of the biologically damaging ultraviolet sunlight (called UV-B), allowing only a **small amount** to reach the Earth's surface. This small amount is used proportionally to make Vitamin D. When an excessive amount of UV-B gets in to the atmosphere because there is a donut hole major issues happen to all life because of UV-B effect by creating heat in the infra red band of sunlight. The absorption of ultraviolet radiation by ozone creates a source of heat. This heat is designed to increase the EZ of water in the clouds. In the Southern hemisphere this does not happen because there is no ozone in the donut hole. This is why their water is "less structured to carry energy. The lack of ozone limits the EZ of water in clouds. Ozone naturally forms the stratosphere itself by changing UV-B radiation to infra red energy to increase the EZ of water. Now go back and think about the perihelion..........it is allowing more radiation in from the sun because the sun is closest to the Earth at this day every year in its revolution. The stratosphere is a region of the atmosphere in which the temperature rises as one goes to higher altitudes, which is not something one expects. It is due to the law of conservation of energy where ozone changes UV-B light to infra red heat and this heat energizes water. This energized water should fall on the living land and its creatures to transfer the energy to them. In the Southern hemisphere this can not happen. Ozone thus plays a key role in the temperature structure of the Earth's atmosphere, more so in the Southern than in the Northern hemisphere.

The water in the southern hemisphere lacks electrons and electric charge.

more UV radiation -> more F and Cl free radicals generated from CFC's
F and Cl free rads mop up electrons becoming F- and Cl- anions
F- and Cl- dissolve in water
Water falls on Australia

This is why we rarely see clouds in the skies in this part of the world especially in summer....there is much less structured water.

How to make structured water:
1)Take some RO water (sourced from some mountain in Aus) and pour it in to a glass jug.
2) Put in the fridge, let it cool (becomes more structured). This is similar to CTing our bodies lol.
3) Sometime later, we take it out and ground the water(by using some sort of metal wire with one end in the water and one end in the earth-you can do this in your garden).
4)Meanwhile, your jug of water will be outside in the Australian sun absorbing all those infra-red radiation which helps structure the water. (photoelectric effect)
5) Before you drink, stir the water\* like a madwoman/man to create a vortex (adds more structure)
6) Voila, you have created structured water aka fountain of youth. Savour your hard work.
7) Pray your BUN/Creatinine falls

### nonchalant — 2014-02-19T16:17:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aussies-and-the-donut-hole.8115/post-111175>

The donut hole issue might be reflected elsewhere, as part of a fractal universe.
Water is the transmission for the various engines of the body. Water moves energy created by mitochondria away to other parts of the cell. When skin is exposed to sunlight, water moves that energy also. (Insert some voodoo for magnetism here. It has infinite range, so it should be affecting all parts of the body...)

The energy dynamics in our atmosphere is also transmitted down to the surface with water. In most places, precipitation carries a good charge that you might actually feel on your skin (to be transferred again using the water transmission scheme.) Near the South Pole, and some other places that have a weaker ozone layer, the energy dynamics in the atmosphere are different. And those differences change the nature of the energy precipitated down with water. In this case the rain is electron deficient, so it is not as helpful to life below.

Perhaps it is similar to the shallow well we have. It has very acid water, so it is deficient in hydrogen. I think that means that the unshared electron pairs that water normally has because of the combination of hydrogen and oxygen, that normally bond with additional hydrogen, are absent.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aussies-and-the-donut-hole.8115/post-111498>

**I still don't know why [Reverse Osmosis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reverse_osmosis) water is better than spring or mineral water. (Aussie or European)**

According to this [link](http://espwaterproducts.com/about-reverse-osmosis.htm) reverse osmosis will remove almost all the beneficial minerals such as zinc, magnesium, selenium, manganese etc etc. From evolutionary perspective we have always drank water with minerals in it. I appreciate we get most minerals from food but surely getting additional from water is a good thing. Although apparently trace minerals in water can also bind to poisons.

Also the **VOCs** - Volatile Organic Chemicals. VOCs are synthetic chemicals dissolved in water—such as insecticides or herbicides are smaller than water molecule and according to [this](http://ianrpubs.unl.edu/pages/publicationD.jsp?publicationId=1109) are not removed by RO membrane. This article was written in 2008 and todays home ro filters are multi-stage.

Particularly if the source (scheme) water for the RO process contains high levels of fluoride, chloride, lead, arsenic, phyto-estrogens etc I would have thought that spring water that is more 'natural' and sans chloride, sodium fluoride, lead, arsenic would be the number one choice not RO water.

What about the water contained in local produce, will the hallides in them from the rainfall negatively offset the lack of halides I ingest via aussie spring water.

[Pro RO link](http://crystalclear-systems.com/the-truth-about-reverse-osmosis-is-safe-drink-water/)

[Negative RO Link](http://www.waterbenefitshealth.com/reverse-osmosis-water.html)

It would be great if somebody compiled FAQs under topics such as water, hormones
eg.
What is the best water to drink?...answer...justification

A mammoth and ongoing task I'm sure Misty and the team have considered it, I think maybe it was mentioned elsewhere and Misty has her fortcoming water blog post (minefield!)

### PaulG — 2014-02-22T00:48:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aussies-and-the-donut-hole.8115/post-111603>

There are [different forms of fluoride](http://fluoridedetective.com/types-of-fluoride/) and different forms can be used around the world and within your country.

My point is that due to modern [water fluoridatio](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Water_fluoridation_controversy)n, RO water can still have hydrofluosilicic acid and sodium silicofluoride which is the most toxic form used in modern industry as the RO membrane cannot remove 100%.

Natural mineral/spring water will contain 0% hydrofluosilicic acid and sodium silicofluoride (unless contaminated by run-off) but will contain some natural calcium fluoride (which is least toxic) depending on what levels are in the ground they are sourced from.

So, context is key so best thing to do is test if possible the RO water in your area and the mineral/spring water. You can then do a side by side comparison to see which is best. It may well be true that RO water always contains less hallogens that mineral water as 95-98% removal of 1 part per million is less than what is naturally in mineral/spring water.

### Da-mo — 2016-01-24T22:41:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aussies-and-the-donut-hole.8115/post-187293>

Something like over 70 or 80% of bottled water in New Zealand comes from the Blue Spring at Putaruru. [http://www.tewaihouwater.co.nz/](http://www.tewaihouwater.co.nz/)

This water fell as rain some 80-100years ago before emerging from the Mamaku ranges aquifier at the Blue Spring. Hopefully it therefore predates the UV/Halides contamination.

> Rainfall from the surrounding bush clad Mamaku ranges moves rapidly through surface sandy soils. It then flows deep into aquifers formed by the ancient eruptions that created Lake Rotorua. Eventually, some 80-100 years later, our water arrives naturally filtered at the Blue Spring.

> Temp. at source: 51.8°F (11.0°C)
> 129TDS
> 6.8ph factor
> 12.43Hardness
> 2.62Calcium
> 5.7Chloride
> 1.47Magnesium
> 0.54Nitrate
> 3.86Potassium
> 77.8Silica
> 8.52Sodium
> milligrams per liter (mg/l)

Hmmm . . . . 5.7mg/l Chloride - is that a problem?

It flows at 42 cubic meters per minute and is 11degreesC year round - and YES, you can swim in it

Hi everyone, I'm new to the forum but not new to Dr K's work . I have red through this thread a few times. I live between Canberra and the South Coast. We swim in a river everyday and that is how I bathe and the beginning of my CT (not so cold at the moment but it is getting colder everyday).
The water issue was perplexing me until I read the blogs that DrK suggested throughout this thread and now I believe I have the idea. I get that RO is the only safe water to drink here and that it needs to be structured for the body to get the most out of it. So I'm fairly happy with myself because this is exactly what we have already been doing.

I use the Apollo Vortexes because I don't have running water, but when we finally build our house we will get an inline system.

I usually spin an entire box of Pureau water at once, while reading a blog or watching a video, and then refill once that gets low.

[http://www.talknetwork.com/2016-05-...is-causing-skin-cancer-not-the-sun-video.html](http://www.talknetwork.com/2016-05-23-sunscreen-is-killing-the-coral-sea-life-is-causing-skin-cancer-not-the-sun-video.html)

Now why is the donut hole and water linked to the entire continents water supply?

Read the current blog on Kawasaki's disease. Can a nnEMF in the atmosphere do something to matter to chain it below our perception to cause a disease that people in Oz get often like AI's and melanoma. Aren't these diseases related to low surface quantum yield effects of sunlight? Yep. So let us dive deeper for the people down under.

The structure of a single water molecule is easy to know: H2O. But how I know water and what is on the surface of OZ is why they are my canary in the coal mine. From 5 pairs of electrons, one pair is localized near the oxygen nucleus and the remaining 4 pairs are "socialized"between protons and oxygen nucleus. The oxygen nucleus partly attracts to the electrons, moving them away from the hydrogen nuclei. This allows hydrogen to become a quantum mechanical Maxwell demon because the latter acquires a weak positive charge. The other two corners of this virtual electron tetrahedron acquire a weak negative charge nearest the oxygen atom. The 2 pairs of electrons are polarized and directed toward the peaks of the tetrad opposite the protons. These unshared pairs of electrons have a crucial role in generation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds. Hydrogen bonds then continuously form and disrupt instantaneously (flickering existence) giving water a polymer like surface. As such it has a high surface tension, high specific heat, high vaporization heat and a high dielectric constant. When you add in the quantum mechanical calculations of physics, the equations say the valence angle in water molecules between the O-H bond have to be 90 degrees. It turns out in reality, the valence angle is 105 degrees because in liquid water, due to the strong polarity off the O-H bonds the minimal repulsion of the positively charge hydrogen atoms increases the angle. As a result the long hydrogen bond (0.28nm) in water has an electrostatic nature and a comparatively weak energy of 14.2-20.9k joules. This electrostatic action makes water very sensitive to EMF's. It makes the water structure very labile and sensitive to different environmental factors. This is why I am convinced OZ's electromagnetic environment is being memorized by water in its surrounding region and why life is ill there. I mentioned it on last night's Q & A. The structure of liquid water int he environment is being continuously changed from moment to moment as it forms. The character of such changes depends wholly on the physical and chemical characteristics of the environmental medium. Research has actually showed us that even keeping dead water (distilled) in a constant medium has its structure changed depending on its age of being dead. (Stepanyan et al, 1999) When you understand water as I do, water's molecular structure before it ever gets to our cells is the perfect quantum fluid that acts as a mirror for the environment it is forced to exist in. The structure of water is therefore like a Ferry boat that carries the "memories of the environment" to our cells to give them information data and energy buried in absorbed light frequencies. This completely explains why Montagnier findings were found........but few, including Pollack see water as I do. It is an adapter of photo-electric and nnEMF that connects the environment to a cell.

Now back to the link above............does sunscreen affect that chemical structure of things below it....Yep. How? It alters the spectrum of sunlight on the surface casting a shadow of darkness on things below doesn't it? Want to bio hack this: Put sunscreen in water and note where it goes? It floats on the surface doesn't it? It acts like a quantum tarp over the surface of water to limit or lower the quantum yield of sunlight to things below. What did the link above says is happening in the sea?

When you understand the above: What do you think sun screen does to your cell water (EZ) underneath it?

BOOM

So the person from OZ who asked me the question of what should we consider doing down under.........now you understand my answer completely.

### CatherineHFD — 2018-10-07T12:35:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aussies-and-the-donut-hole.8115/post-252555>

I assume you're referring to the carbonated/sparkling version of waters .. I will find the book for you and quote him .. trust me I'm disappointed too, because when accepting invitations to be at a bar for example, and I drink little if any wine any more (it spins me out too much, although I wouldn't turn down 1/2 glass of something really special), I relied on being able to at least order a sparkling mineral water .. now there's pretty much nothing, unless i know for a fact the bubbles are natural, not carbonated. So here it is: "Carbonated Beverages: Carbonated CO2 is a deadly waste product of metabolism .. it's carried by the blood to the lungs where it's exchanged for incoming oxygen and then expelled with the outgoing breath. The average adult exhales enough CO2 and other poisons of metabolism in a 24 hr period to kill 12 adult elephants. CO2 is a deadly gas and a great number of people have died from CO2 poisoning. As this gas is heavier than air, it sinks to low places and well bottoms. There are many cases on record where people have gone down into an old well and died instantly from excess CO2." Dr Flannagan goes on about how dangerous CO2 is ..

I guess it all comes down to how much you drink, because like with anything, it's about the individual's body and it's ability to buffer toxicity, and for how long before it becomes serious ..

With regard to distilled .. I am not saying I'm stopping entirely with what I'm doing, just that I'm now including some apparently lower-D water into the mix ..

### Anya — 2018-10-08T06:17:29+0300

### Benjamin Stone — 2018-11-13T19:59:04+0200

Hi,

In older threads I see statements by Dr. Kruse more emphatically opposed to distilled water. I have been experimenting with Divinia Water, which is double distilled - but also lower in deuterium and containing relatively abundant EZ. The family who runs the small company has an impressive list of testimonials from various people with health issues benefiting from their water. So is it just the effect of the DDW and EZ, overriding the potential harm from the distillation? Or one heck of a placebo effect? Its too early for me to report on anything substantive, but I think the water is helping me with regularity in terms of bowel movements. I drink no more than 500ml a day.

I will also add the owner has said the water will dissolve plastic and must be sold in glass!

Over three liters a day= diabetes insipidus=not healthy
\------------------
One of the pituitary hormones is vasopressin
ADH-AntiDiureticHormone
vasopressin
desmopressin
\------------------------------------------
Being thirsty is response to ADH going low lower.
Being constantly thirsty --->possibly ADH is to low.
When thirst comes and you do not drink right away but wait 20-30 minutes, ADH may go up and thirst may go away.
If you do that every day you may drink less daily on average.

You will not get dehydrated or be short on water as long as you have fat on your body (or eat fat)
because when water is need,
mitochondria will make up what is need.
(In extreme mitochondria is able to make 10liters per hour)

This is discussed by dr Boros in his video.
Look up attached file with link to that video and my notes.
He take it to extreme, he drinks 250cc=1/4liter a day

Note that inducing mitochondria to produce matrix water results in variety of highly significant benefits
and
that those benefits are missing when one drinks too much (which suppresses mitochondrial production).
\---------------
You can remove 5ppm in one "freezing" of water.
That is significant when you compare it to some expensive waters.
But the freezing process have to be done carefully.
Get thermometer
put it into bottle of water to be "cooled", place it in the freezer
remove from freezer when
water temperature is real close to zero
but
water is still liquid
remove from freezer
shake it (you will see little ice forming)
drain thru sieve, remove ice
that ice have a high deuterium content
and
it is very little of it (as it should be).

> When thirst comes and you do not drink right away but wait 20-30 minutes, ADH may go up and thirst may go away.
> If you do that every day you may drink less daily on average.
>
> You will not get dehydrated or be short on water as long as you have fat on your body (or eat fat)
> because when water is need,
> mitochondria will make up what is need.
> (In extreme mitochondria is able to make 10liters per hour)
>
> This is discussed by dr Boros in his video.
> Look up attached file with link to that video and my notes.
> He take it to extreme, he drinks 250cc=1/4liter a day
>
> Note that inducing mitochondria to produce matrix water results in variety of highly significant benefits
> and
> that those benefits are missing when one drinks too much (which suppresses mitochondrial production).
> \---------------
> You can remove 5ppm in one "freezing" of water.
> That is significant when you compare it to some expensive waters.
> But the freezing process have to be done carefully.
> Get thermometer
> put it into bottle of water to be "cooled", place it in the freezer
> remove from freezer when
> water temperature is real close to zero
> but
> water is still liquid
> remove from freezer
> shake it (you will see little ice forming)
> drain thru sieve, remove ice
> that ice have a high deuterium content
> and
> it is very little of it (as it should be).
>
>
> [View attachment 9553](https://forum.jackkruse.com/attachments/9553/)

by the way .. I haven't been drinking that much because I'm thirsty .. only because it's what I've learned from information I've picked up from people I thought knew what they are talking about. even in Summer .. I struggle a little sometimes getting 2 litres down, so this is great news .. also keeping the distiller going is one less job to be constantly monitoring. So do understand you in that you can remove 5ppm in one freezing, and that can be repeated as much as you have time for to remove another 5ppm, etc, etc .. maybe a number of times? thanks

### JanSz — 2018-11-15T14:18:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aussies-and-the-donut-hole.8115/post-255656>

> [CatherineHFD said:](/goto/post?id=255643)

Fish bone broth should raise ADH (and reduce thirst).
\----
About 5ppm in one freezing.

This information comes from what my friend in Poland did.
The testing was done by company
Eurofin
That is international company. So it is also in AU.
I was talking about that in another thread.
[https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.p...o-you-buy-this-50ppm.20927/page-8#post-239806](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/how-to-make-ddw-do-you-buy-this-50ppm.20927/page-8#post-239806)
He tested only the water that he froze once.
And the result was 145.084ppm
Actually they do not give results in ppm(D/H)
they provide δX
δX=(-68.54)
He also tested Qlarivia
But he did not tested the water before freezing.
So I only assumed that his water is 150ppm (as it likely is)(but no proof).

The freezing that he did removes minute amounts of water so if one have time to do it,
it can be repeated many times.

### NDC74 — 2019-03-15T20:10:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/aussies-and-the-donut-hole.8115/post-265585>

> [Nick said:](/goto/post?id=94566)
>
> Hi Caroline
>
> I need to catch up on the Q&A on this one, sounds like there have been some bombshells going off. Low Carb, Epi-Paleo and CT all year doesn't sound like too much of head bender for us in Australia. Thanks Jack, for throwing a bit more love to us Aussies!
>
> To answer your question Caroline, The Magnetico is awesome! We have two now, one 10G for my wife and I, and 1 5G for our 3yo. he has been the main driver for the magnetico as Jack gave us some pretty focussed advice a several months ago about how to tackle his massive list of allergies and eczema. Turns out he was sleeping next to all of our wireless devices on the other side of the wall and a big constant 12mgauss constant field from a fridge. We have removed as much emf as possible, esp at night, all wifi is gone, all phones turned off, no electrical devices turned on in any bed room, fridge has been moved and magneticos purchased. We also turn off lots of lights after sun down, eat early and feed the kids lots of fatty meats wherever possible. I would like to say we do this perfectly but our experience with a 3yo falls short from time to time, bananas in winter ahhhhhh. At least he eats heaps of good quality meat.
>
> In three months we have seen his skin clear significantly, we are using around 1/4 of the steroid creams and in his last batch of testing we won back several food groups and other allergens. We still have a way to go with the nuts and eggs but we are feeling hopeful. Our n=1 experience with the Magnetico and a 3yo is that it takes time to adjust. My wife slept with hime for about 3 months as he was agitated during the night and was wriggling in his sleep a lot. She made sure he didn't start scratching and open up his skin. We persisted because even though the sleeps were tough, he showed positive signs quickly, for instance when his skin became infected he didn't need antibiotics, for the first time he was able to fight off the infection himself, his skin started clearing and he generally looked much more calm during the day. We gave the Magnetico a break for a about three weeks where his sleep is now much better than it has ever been and we are about to start up again. After talking to the guys at magnetico we will put it under his bed which will drop it to 2G and look to bring it back up to 5G as soon as we can. Hopefully this brings us the best of both worlds.
>
> My wife and I love the 10G one we have on our bed, it is like the 'sleep machine', not much else to report as we are both pretty healthy and don't have complicated health issues that we are battling (at leasts that we know about, we will start testing ourselves soon). We just now need to get another 5G one for our 6yo. Expensive, but as they don't wear out or break down they are a crazy great value investment over a lifetime considering the benefits they bring.
>
> Hope this experience helps anyone dealing with eczema and allergies, it is a terrible thing to deal with as a family, physically and emotionally. Everyone has opinions and we tend to be frowned upon for not doing enough by people on the outside looking in, even though we have been at our wits ends. The specialists here in Brisbane just offer advice on creams and wrapping which changes in every consult. This has been extremely frustrating. Dr Kruses explanations however have been like a life raft thrown our way with concrete actions we can do, even though I don't understand a lot of the detail yet. The results are starting to come through and if this trend continues then we will have achieved a remarkable outcome.

G’day, Nick.
Thanks for sharing your experience in Magnetico. What is the thickness of your matress? The reason I asked is that my matress is 30 cm thick and according to Magnetico, the strength of the magnet will be about 20% less. Im not sure now if that will still be effective.

May be worth checking.

...

Take only meat discard the rest.
Lots of Asian ladies would pick up the heads and bones that otherwise would be dumped out for crabs to eat.

For someone living near marina this may be free resource.

I do not want to eat my pets, at least not yet.
Last year we had real chicken. They lay eggs and we ate them.
In my neighborhood having chicken is problematic, but our neighbors are good and do not complain.
The real chickens are noisy, but especially roosters. So we gave away roosters.
This year my chickens are silky chicken. They are quiet.
For now, I do not even know which are roosters. Likely because they are still too young.
Birds have a ball. They love silky chicken feathers for their nests. Those feathers are really fluffy.

# Thread: Light bulbs
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-bulbs.8116/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AnnieW — 2013-08-31T02:37:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-bulbs.8116/post-94408>

Besides candlelight and amber bulbs, are there any other light sources which could be safely used in the home? Husband has put the kibosh on amber bulbs (surprise surprise) and wants to stick with neutral/white light.

### nonchalant — 2013-08-31T03:20:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-bulbs.8116/post-94418>

I've been buying 25 watt bulbs. If a fixture has 4 bulbs in it, I replace one with a 25 watt. Then in a month or so, I replace another. DH's night vision was really bad, but it seems to be improving with better nutrition. Since it is summer, I have been more lenient with light in the evening. In winter I will be much more strict.

Also, I use 60 watt yellow bug lights at the kitchen stove and in the refrigerator. Perhaps your husband, if he doesn't cook much, won't mind that. If I could find lower-wattage bug lights I'd snap them up pronto.

### AnnieW — 2013-08-31T18:38:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-bulbs.8116/post-94494>

Thanks for the suggestions. The good thing is my husband really doesn't like light (vampire) so he doesn't like bright bulbs at all. Most in the house are 40 watt. I need to get more anyway so I will see about buying the 25 watt. I have a huge fluorescent light in my kitchen that I'd rather do without. Unfortunately we rent so I can't go changing fixtures.

My husband only comes in the kitchen to forage for snacks and ask me what's for dinner so I don't think he really cares about kitchen lighting, LOL.

### ste — 2013-08-31T14:29:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-help-with-labs.8119/post-94464>

Hi all,

I decided to sign up and post the labs I got back last week. They are looking better than they used to but I am wondering if anything is wrong. 28 year old male. I have been pretty strict about diet & sleep over the last year.

The low pregnenolone is disconcerting and indicates poor mitochondrial functioning. Already taking Ubiquinol & RALA/ALC. I am also looking to lower the homocysteine and wondering about the low Free T3/RT3 ratio (assuming I calculated it correctly).

We decided to supplement 50mg pregnenolone in addition to other hormones I am taking (25mg DHEA, testosterone gel, low dose growth hormone, 25mg cortisol)

Thanks!

Code:

```
Vitamin B12            676  ng/L    300-835
Erythrocyte folate     274  ng/mL   140-628
Ferritin               160  mg/L    50-240

BUN                    47   mg/dL   10-50
Creatinine             1.22 mg/dL   0.70-1.30

NOTE: different lab reference ranges, BUN is upper end of normal, should be equivalent to US BUN of ~19
BUN/Creatinine should be equivalent to ~15.6 (normal range 10-20)

Fasting Glucose        89   mg/dL   60-110
Insulin                5.8  mUI/L   3-22

Sodium                 145  mmol/L  135-145
Calcium                2.48 mmol/L  2.05-2.65

25-hydroxy-vitamin D + 66.8 ng/mL   30.0-60.0

Triglycerides          82   mg/dL   30-170
Total cholesterol    + 220  mg/dL   120-200
HDL cholesterol        70   mg/dL   40-109
LDL cholesterol      + 134  mg/dL   2.8-4.97

O6/O3 ratio            2.74         1.0-4.0
LA/DGLA ratio        + 11.09        <=4.40

hs-CRP               + 0.59         0-0.55
```

Code:

```
Vitamin E              11.6 mg/mL   10.0-19.0
Vitamin A              81   mg/dL   43-111
Selenium               138  mg/L    90-143
Zinc                 + 147  mg/dL   84-135

TSH                    2.94  mE/L   0.3-4.5
Free T3                3.62  pg/mL  2.1-4.2
Reverse T3             0.44  nmol/L 0.14-0.54 (28.57ng/dL)
Free T3/RT3 ratio    - 12.7         > 20
Free T4                1.34  ng/dL  0.7-1.8

Aldosterone        ++  447   pg/mL  40-300

FSH                 -  <0.5  IE/L   2-15
Estradiol              28    ng/L   <30
Estrone                59    pg/mL  10-60

Free Testostorone      239   ng/L   50-280

# Thread: Insomnia and Breakfast
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/insomnia-and-breakfast.8120/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### yorkiegirl — 2013-08-31T14:52:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/insomnia-and-breakfast.8120/post-94466>

Hi, just trying to get going with all the information. Can I check, that as I suffer from severe insomnia, I can be
awake from 1am in the morning, try to relax but can't. Don't get up though until say 6 or 7am, will I be able to get into a circadian cycle having breakfast then, as obviously can't have breakfast as soon as I wake up at that time?

Thank you for any advice

### jib — 2013-08-31T17:39:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/insomnia-and-breakfast.8120/post-94481>

The sleep blog may give you some relief. Also some of the magnesium supplements can help with sleeping if you are getting proper hydration.

### caroline — 2013-09-01T02:10:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/insomnia-and-breakfast.8120/post-94535>

> [fatlass said:](/goto/post?id=94466)
>
> Hi, just trying to get going with all the information. Can I check, that as I suffer from severe insomnia, I can be
> awake from 1am in the morning, try to relax but can't. Don't get up though until say 6 or 7am, will I be able to get into a circadian cycle having breakfast then, as obviously can't have breakfast as soon as I wake up at that time?
>
> Thank you for any advice

Why can't you have breakfast at that time? You need to have a BAB within 30 minutes of rising. That is the critical part. That is what helps to reset your clock.

### Lahelada — 2013-09-01T02:18:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/insomnia-and-breakfast.8120/post-94537>

This is purely my take from my experience. I also used to wake up earlier than what could be considered BAB time although not quite as extreme as 1am. What I would do is the following :

Get up, do not turn on any lights, stay away from lit areas if there are any and just try and stay awake for 20 mins or if you feel sleepy earlier so much the better.

Have the BAB when NEW daylight has reached you and you are still in bed. Don't stay up though,go back to bed however often it takes until you sleep always without light.

I also second taking magnesium and an Epsom salt bath /footbath one hour before bed. They are both magnesium based but I am thinking that it is better to have both delivery methods. Good luck, I know the despair of waking up when everyone just goes to bed.

### caroline — 2013-09-01T02:35:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/insomnia-and-breakfast.8120/post-94543>

Great advise Lahelda .....BTW - I didn't mean to have a BAB at 1:00am. I think you said you get up at 6:00am?

### yorkiegirl — 2013-09-06T13:09:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/insomnia-and-breakfast.8120/post-95015>

Thanks Lahelda, I use magnesium oil and epsom too, this does help relaxing me for sleep on a night too,which is great, it's just the waking up early bit. If I take magnesium then it doesn't have the same effect, if you know what I mean. I try and stay in the dark until sunrise. When I wake up early, I can never fall asleep again, I never feel sleepy. I know this is going to take some doing to sort out.
Caroline - yes if I get up at 6am/or 7am, I get my breakfast within 1/2 hour, and have been doing BAB for a few weeks now.

### Lahelada — 2013-09-07T03:37:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/insomnia-and-breakfast.8120/post-95107>

Do I remember reading that doing light weight work helps with sleep or am I imagining that I read this somewhere here ?
I have been waking up at 4 am every day this week and it is getting a bit old . 5 hours of sleep and maybe one extra right before I need to get up is not my idea of "nice#

### Renfred — 2013-10-11T06:55:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/insomnia-and-breakfast.8120/post-97500>

Hi Yorkiegirl,
Avoid taking naps in day timing and avoid over sleeping to deal with insomnia. Exercise regularly and early morning and create your sleep schedule. Avoid alcohol, smoking and caffeine and make your bedroom comfortable so you will get better sleep.

CHEAPEST Website for BEST Vitamin C? I know Jack uses a lot of Life Extension products but he also has a NOW foods Magnesium Malate on his supplement list but no recommendation for Vitamin C
I have been using NOW Foods Vitamin C with Rose Hips.... but ran out....I was taking extra because trying to do the Iodine protocol....

### Lahelada — 2013-09-01T16:07:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cheapest-website-for-best-vitamin-c.8125/post-94591>

Cheapest Vitamin C is the vitamin c powder used for baking bread or as a preservative for olives. You will have to look around where you can get hold of it. I get mine in a bakery supply store.

### Grantmeliberty — 2013-09-02T08:34:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cheapest-website-for-best-vitamin-c.8125/post-94663>

I have found Iherb.com to be the most reasonable for Vit C powder, either Ascorbic Acid or Sodium Ascorbate. I have been taking about 4 grams of Vit C daily for about 10 years and at 64 I have no problems with my back, although I had had periodic issues before that. I had read where an MD had had chronic lower back pain and it resolved with 4 or 5 grams daily.

### MeghanK — 2013-09-02T17:57:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cheapest-website-for-best-vitamin-c.8125/post-94685>

### RGP — 2013-09-03T14:49:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cheapest-website-for-best-vitamin-c.8125/post-94742>
