### veelansu — 2013-10-24T20:43:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gauging-interest-for-a-weekend-with-jack.8532/post-98898>

The three airports in the area are Pensacola, FL (PNS), Gulfport/Biloxi, MS (GPT) and Mobile, AL (MOB). Mobile is central to either Mississippi or Florida but it is the more pricey one when it comes to tickets. Gulfport tends to have pricing about $100 less than Mobile depending on the date (US Flights). Pensacola is sometimes more and sometimes significantly less than either. If we decide to do a place other than NOLA, these are the three that one would look at to travel into. NOLA is cheaper than all three. Sometimes when traveling to Mob, I just fly into NOLA and drive 1 1/2 hours because the cost is that much better. Something to think about.

### ATL_Paleo — 2013-10-24T21:09:56+0300

Rooms from $ 89 for the weekend of Feb 21 to Feb 23, 2014 .... conference rooms available .... free hotel shuttle from MSY airport which is 3 miles away .... just off I-10 so the Mardi Gras crowds should be minimal for the parades scheduled that weekend.

If the weekend is in NOLA, the part I like the best is what seems to be a nice park across the street ..... not the ocean, but grounding, etc. is possible.

Judging by the deals we are looking into for the weekend before Mardi Gras, I don't think we'll be dealing with a Mardi Gras crowd. It's a good time ... it's after the Super Bowl, before crazy town. We've been able to get the best rates in this window.

>

> [veelansu said:](/goto/post?id=98898)
>
> The three airports in the area are Pensacola, FL (PNS), Gulfport/Biloxi, MS (GPT) and Mobile, AL (MOB). Mobile is central to either Mississippi or Florida but it is the more pricey one when it comes to tickets. Gulfport tends to have pricing about $100 less than Mobile depending on the date (US Flights). Pensacola is sometimes more and sometimes significantly less than either. If we decide to do a place other than NOLA, these are the three that one would look at to travel into. NOLA is cheaper than all three. Sometimes when traveling to Mob, I just fly into NOLA and drive 1 1/2 hours because the cost is that much better. Something to think about.

I love the idea of another place, but ... I've been a part of planning a lot of events and I think we need to go for affordable and convenient for this first one. I don't want expensive flights or lots of driving to deter participation. Fitting an event in one's schedule can be challenging enough as it is without glutting it down with complicated travel schemes. A smaller airport sometimes means way longer travel days for those coming from more than an hour's flight away ... there's just a lot that goes into it. I think NOLA is a solid place to plan the first one with plenty of upside. Plus, it's convenient for Jack, too.

> [SeaHorse said:](/goto/post?id=98953)

### RGP — 2013-10-25T14:48:01+0300

We don't have the disposable income for the other levels of membership. I for one could make this happen if the $800 I am saving for attendance is used for travel expenses.

What is the true intent of this get together: to meet and greet and help as many people as possible, or is this another revenue stream? The attendance will be greater if it is available to the masses. For those of you who are old enough, remember when the VW bug hit the American market.

### cantweight — 2013-10-25T15:43:27+0300

### tellmisty — 2013-10-25T15:54:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gauging-interest-for-a-weekend-with-jack.8532/post-99009>

> [RGP said:](/goto/post?id=99002)
>
> Why don't you gauge the interest if all members were charged $199? There is a reason many or most of the members are bronze club members. We don't have the disposable income for the other levels of membership. I for one could make this happen if the $800 I am saving for attendance is used for travel expenses.
>
> What is the true intent of this get together: to meet and greet and help as many people as possible, or is this another revenue stream? The attendance will be greater if it is available to the masses. For those of you who are old enough, remember when the VW bug hit the American market.
>
> Just a thought.

Putting an event like this on takes money, plain and simple. There is a lot of time that goes into planning, and there are a lot of expenses for the event in general. No one works for free ... it all has to be budgeted. And Jack's time is very valuable, too ... if he's blocking off his schedule to do an event, I have to make sure that there's compensation for him to make it worthwhile. I can't make the math work at $199, plain and simple. As the event grows and we get sponsors for it, maybe that price point will be more feasible.

The event fee covers your pass into the event. Food, hotel and travel are up to each guest (that's just frankly too much for us to plan for each person).

I understand that not everyone can do it, but for those who've been clamoring for it, I'm trying to deliver! Jack gives away TONS to members of this community who never spend a dime, for which we can all be grateful.

### Hope — 2013-10-25T16:47:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gauging-interest-for-a-weekend-with-jack.8532/post-99012>

Yes, this stuff is very expensive and SO much work to put together....ugh. I admire you for being willing to take it on Misty!

My big question would be if there is really time to do everyone's HH and give input on every single person that wants it...if there are 25 people and we have all day on Saturday = 8 hours, that gives 19.2 minutes a person and the time would have to be very strict. I think managing expectations is key too so that people aren't disappointed. It was very hard when I got all my work done for a consult and then....boom.

And I think having people get their own hotels or lodging is perfect too - there are always discounts to be had with enough notice, and there is airbnb and Craigslist. People could get together and rent a large home with several bedrooms and kitchens and not have to pay more than $50 a night maybe.

### ATL_Paleo — 2013-10-25T18:01:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gauging-interest-for-a-weekend-with-jack.8532/post-99019>

Hope, you make some excellent points. I understand there are costs for planning a meeting. Each of us must weigh ever the perceived benefits of the weekend are worth the cost. ....... To date, the only benefits that have been posted are the chance to meet Jack (very nice), the opportunity to meet other Kruisers in person, and the opportunity to have a Consult w/ Jack (for an so far undisclosed additional fee).

After reading your post and thinking, I wonder aloud how Misty and Jack will be able to provide value to match the cost of $ 499 to $ 899 plus travel and lodging. I would love to meet Jack, but I wonder if that is worth about $ 1,000 to me.

Your comment that really resonated w/ me was TIME. I agree that having 25 or more people for just a weekend will probably not be enough time to accomplish things that most people would want to do.

I guess the next step is for Misty and team to post an agenda ...... so we can evaulate if our personal goals are likely to be achieve at a GO weekend.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-10-25T19:02:56+0300

# Thread: NBA stars find less playing time is more healthy
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nba-stars-find-less-playing-time-is-more-healthy.8535/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ATL_Paleo — 2013-10-23T00:37:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nba-stars-find-less-playing-time-is-more-healthy.8535/post-98623>

[http://bleacherreport.com/articles/...tars-play-less-and-for-good-reason-heres-why?](http://bleacherreport.com/articles/1815776-in-todays-nba-even-the-stars-play-less-and-for-good-reason-heres-why?)

Interesting article on NBA players. A number of the protocols from the blog at being used by some athletes.
\------------------------------
More Rest, Better Recovery

One of the significant effects of deeper benches: allowing players to recover better game to game.

"Not only are you developing your team using your entire roster, but you're also giving your guys more time to rest," Cartwright said. "It's not a sprint, it's a marathon, and the healthier that you can stay during the course of a season, the better."

Clark added, "The less minutes played by these athletes will certainly increase their durability, resilience and longevity because it enhances recovery overall. When you play less minutes, you get less beat up."

Fratello said that like in Major League Baseball, where managers are more protective of their pitchers and use them in fewer innings, NBA coaches have made a more conscious effort to limit their players' minutes. He said that's even happening overseas, where he's been the head coach of the Ukraine national basketball team for the past three summers.

"Now, people are afraid to play guys 42, 44 minutes a night," he said. "It's just part of the times, part of the expanding of the staffs. There are that many more people involved, and you have that many more opinions. And they think this guy will last longer in the NBA if we cut down his minutes to 34, 36 minutes a night."

Along with the minutes decrease, players have the luxury of recuperating with advanced technology and treatment methods. Those aim to help players maintain their minutesâ€”even while lowerâ€”more consistently throughout the entire season. Cartwright recalled that when he played for the Bulls and New York Knicks in the 1980s and '90s, a player who hurt his knee would just apply some ice, take some aspirin and then put on a brace for the following game.

He said that's all "completely different today."

"We're smarter in how we train and play," Cartwright said. "It's so unbelievable with what's happening now with how players are being taken care of after the game with the trainers. You've got Jacuzzis, cold tubs, masseuses, oxygen tanks for the guys and cryotherapy that freezes the players. We didn't have any of that when I came into the league."

# Thread: Will this really work for me?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/will-this-really-work-for-me.8536/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 22
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### GoldFishie — 2013-10-23T02:18:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/will-this-really-work-for-me.8536/post-98663>

Hello all. My name is Jan (age 61) and I'm from Atlanta. I have many years of a crapped out PVN and have exhausted the best alternative help that I can access. So here I land and am considering 'taking the plunge' but since my diet is so extremely limited, I'm wondering if it will work for me.

I have started to assiduously follow no lights/electronics after sundown, grounding for at least an hour a day, all the EMF stuff - had SmartMeter removed, etc., sleep in completely dark BR, drink 2 gallons RO water/day, eat within 30 minutes of waking up and then every 2.5-3 hours throughout the day. Hammer DHA/fish oils, curcumin, etc. From all appearances AI although never positive on TPO. Stage 3 kidney disease. Was on steroids from age 7-17. Tried to take Tirosint and for about 3 weeks life was wonderful. Then bam I just got plain pissy and could not tolerate it. Can't handle even an entsy tiny amount of DHEA cream. I pretty much go around in first gear or flat on my back. Up twice during the night to urinate, have to drink during the night, sleep on average 7 hours. Genesis 1:2 bowels (without form and void).

5'10" 145#

As to diet ~ I read what Dr. Kruse recommends. I have never had an oyster in my life, I am allergic to shellfish, have never been an organ meat eater, fish makes me hyper. Allergic to eggs, dairy & coconut. I hammer olive oil. So there goes all of the primo foods he suggests. He says no fowl and that is the staple of my diet, plus grass feed beef. Beef tallow nauseates me. I can handle duck fat. I do eat a small amount of either sweet potato, winter squash or apple with a meal. Tons of vegetables. Obviously cannot do BAB. Have mastered kraut but now cannot handle any salt. So there it sits . . . If I eat any salt my arms go numb at night. Can't handle water keifer or kombucha.

I have a mesolimbic windup but am safe to be on the playground as my frontal lobe is intact.

You know, I'd like to be special in other ways in life!

I have really resisted going this route especially with the recommended CT because I'm frickin freezing all of the time. Face I would be willing to start with.

I have access to any and all labs no problem. It's just the diet I'm concerned about.

Well, the sun is setting so I must disappear. Looking forward to some encouragement from ya'll in the a.m.!

~jan

### caroline — 2013-10-23T02:40:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/will-this-really-work-for-me.8536/post-98666>

Welcome Gold! Sounds like you have made a pretty good start.

Why are you eating so often? A lot of folks here are very limited with their diet and they manage.

Have you read Jack's book? Listened to his podcasts? The webinars and follow up Q&A are pure gold. When you read a blog make very sure that you read all the comments too. Dr. K. Answers all questions at the end of a blog.

Have you read the easy start guides on the home page?

Please start a journal in the journal section so Dr. Kruse can get some idea about you - he needs context.

This is doable ....however ... It will take time. Yoy didn't get broken overnite and this isn't a linear process ....but if you are committed and 100% all in - 6 months from now and a year from now you won't recognize yourself...

Tell us a little bit more about your life, do you have children? If so - how were the pregnancies?

### caroline — 2013-10-23T02:45:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/will-this-really-work-for-me.8536/post-98667>

The journal section....

Go to the top of this page and click on forum and go down 5 categories until you come to the optimal reset section and journals. Then start a new thread.

I will watch for you there! If you have a look at other's journals it is helpful to see what others are struggling with ....and you can help others too. Everyone has different life experiences and we all try to help one another....

Please keep asking questions....Dr k. Doesn't know what you don't know unless you ask...

We look forward to following your journey..... It is a ride of a life time!

### caroline — 2013-10-23T02:50:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/will-this-really-work-for-me.8536/post-98669>

Please post your labs in the lab section.....and someone will have a look....

Remember Dr K. Or anyone else can't help much unless they have context....

### Jack Kruse — 2013-10-23T04:41:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/will-this-really-work-for-me.8536/post-98694>

1.My name is Jan (age 61) and I'm from Atlanta. I have many years of a crapped out PVN (Can you say EMF anyone?)
2.As to diet ~ I read what Dr. Kruse recommends. I have never had an oyster in my life, I am allergic to shellfish, have never been an organ meat eater, fish makes me hyper. Allergic to eggs, dairy & coconut. I hammer olive oil. So there goes all of the primo foods he suggests. He says no fowl and that is the staple of my diet, plus grass feed beef. Beef tallow nauseates me. I can handle duck fat. I do eat a small amount of either sweet potato, winter squash or apple with a meal. Tons of vegetables. Obviously cannot do BAB. Have mastered kraut but now cannot handle any salt. So there it sits . . . If I eat any salt my arms go numb at night. Can't handle water keifer or kombucha. (No wonder you are struggling.......no DC current)

Key take home: RO water, ketogenic diet, Read the quantum electron blog, hormone 101, 102, EE7. The biggie from EE 7:Why do humans need iodine at its core compared to its nearest ancestor the chimps?

It turns our sufficient dietary iodine is crucial for proper ketogenesis in our liver and in our brain for full immune and neural development. Ketogenesis is needed to make a hominid brain at a foundational level as you learned in Energy and Epigenetics 1 blog. Iodine however, does a lot more to humans than most realize.

IODINE, ESTROGEN, and KETOSIS: THE WAKE UP CALL FOR MENOPAUSAL WOMEN

Iodine is the link back to autoimmunity and the neurogenesis connection of the MHC 1 gene. Iodine absorption falls in the human gut when estrogen levels rise from any cause. This helps explain why women have much higher rates of autoimmune diseases like Multiple Sclerosis and hypothyroidism than men. It also explains why women have less myelin formation than men in adulthood. Remember from Energy and Epigenetics 1 we must be ketotic to make myelin in humans. Women have less iodine absorption by design. Women have higher estrogen levels to bear children. Lower levels of myelination allow women to be “more sensitive” to environmental triggers to pass that information to their offspring’s DNA. Myelination, also happens to be a proxy for mammalian regeneration. This was proven by Robert O. Becker. This now explains why women also make T2 thyroid hormone from their ovaries and breasts. This helps them offset their decrease ability to absorb iodine from their guts.

BIOLOGY GEEKS: Until recent years, T2, because of its very low affinity for thyroid hormone receptors (THR), was considered an inactive metabolite of thyroid hormones. However, several recent studies indicate that T2 is more important than originally thought. In fact, T2 is necessary for production of the deiodinase enzyme that converts the less active T4 into the potent T3 in the body. Early studies on diiodothyronine revealed its ability to stimulate cellular/mitochondrial respiration during the activation of the Pentose phosphate fat burning pathway by a receptor-independent pathway. Mitochondrial and energy-releasing mechanisms seem to be major targets of T2, although outside the mitochondria T2 also has effects on carriers, ion-exchangers, and enzymes.

Significant increases in the liver actions of glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase and malic enzyme were found in studies cited below. These enzymes are
necessary for fat metabolism and liberation of energy in the form of beta oxidation. T2 exhibits significant increases in Growth Hormone release from the pituitary have been found in studies. Both T2 and T3 increased Growth Hormone release by 5-fold. This makes sense when you consider women need growth hormone to stimulate their breast and ovarian tissue for fertility and reproduction.

NON GEEKS: In women, iodine is also critical in making breast milk, tears, and saliva. The higher your estrogen level, or the lower your SHBG level, the more likely your eyes, mouth, skin, and vagina will be dry. You also won’t make a lot of breast milk to feed a child. Your ability to sweat will also be altered.

I personally believe this is why women go through menopause now. It is because of their evolutionary design. No one seems to have a clue why menopause exists. I think I do. Women need to lower their estrogen levels as they age, to reclaim their total body iodine levels, to help them have a longer lifespan as they age by increasing their ability to myelinate to increase their regenerative DC current by increasing their iodine absorption. This also helps explain why diabetic women have a higher incidence of peripheral neuropathy than men do. They have less myelin, so any further decline in iodine assimilation impairs ketogenesis to regenerate myelin and diminishes their ability to heal and regenerate. This is where the decreased immunity seen in diabetes rears its ugly head for those with metabolic syndrome.

This also helps explain why women in menopause get hot flashes and night sweats. Iodine stimulates uncoupling proteins and it stimulates the sweat glands. When they had their menstrual period, they did not have the stimulatory effect of iodine, but now without their cycle they do, rather suddenly. It is not from a lack of estrogen, as most physicians believe, it is from more iodine in their bodies. Sweating is a new evolutionary design in hominids. Primates do not have the sweat gland we do. It is a change to the mammalian body plan unique to humans. This was done to be able to cool our bodies down to save energy, because they work better cooled down because it increases semiconducting currents. Primates use vitamin C as an endogenous antioxidant but humans evolved to use iodine as their peripheral antioxidant. We have large brains so we have a lot more semiconductors to cool everywhere on our body. Iodine also helps lower the oxidation of DHA in synapses in humans. This is why the brain has its own thyroid hormone control system because we have way more semiconduction circuits in our brain. When we lose our iodine function in the brain we lose the ability to offset some of the inflammatory cytokines in the brain circuits. In the frontal lobes this causes ADHD or depression and in the leptin receptors it causes an inability to sense energy balance and leads to obesity. When humans become energy inefficient they usually gain weight, as I laid out in EMF 2 and recently in Energy and Epigenetics 4, using Kleiber’s law.

Sweating is another buffer that we use to become more energy efficient, before we need to expand out fat mass to have the same effect. This is why women gain fat mass in menopause too. It happens because they are less energy efficient because of their loss of progesterone and prolactin. This reflex sweating, seen in humans, is done to cool down women’s new found rediscovery of efficient semiconduction, as their estrogen levels fall in menopause. Once they acclimate to their new increased iodine absorption, their symptoms resolve because they adapt by increasing their myelination and their DC current improves. Many menopausal women, get placed upon estrogen and sometimes their symptoms of hot flashes goes away, but so does their ability to reclaim iodine to myelinate. This implies they can alter their immune balance as they age. Iodine happens to increase neurogenesis in humans, so when iodine is low cognitive haze is also a result. The brain has its own thyroid hormone system to control neurogenesis even if the body stores are low. This is an example of how the brain controls energy partitioning for itself over vegetative systems in the thyroid gland. This patterns what we see in atoms too.

So if you get placed upon estrogen, or happen to be estrogen dominant for any reason at all, male or female, you may get cognitive haze. Iodine increases our ability to become ketotic to myelinate and regenerate our immune system and our brain because of the MHC 1 evolutionary connection. If you do not eat a ketotic diet when these changes happen these benefits will be hidden from you. For most of the blogosphere they remain a mystery.

### caroline — 2013-10-23T08:12:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/problem-with-my-laptop.8539/post-98708>

I just took my laptop in to get fixed. It seemed like the track pad is going. It was replaced 3 months ago.

The tech just called me and told me I had spilled something on it......something orange and sticky....like soda...

I have not spilled anything on it and certainly not soda! I am the only one that uses it. My grandsons haven't been over in ages and I still would not have soda here.

What else could it possibly be? Is it possible when they fixed it someone spilled something? It never seemed 100% when I got it back ...but nothing significant....I said nothing.

Thanks for any help/ ideas you could give me....
It is a MacBook pro and I have the care plan and there is a 3 month warranty from when they fixed it.

I suppose they will think that I am fibbing and really did spill something. My understanding - if you spill something it invalidates the warranty.

**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### HoneyChild — 2013-10-23T19:10:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/steel-reinforced-concrete-homes-and-emf.8542/post-98745>

One of the houses we've been thinking of buying is built using the Dorran method of construction. Basically after the war thousands of these homes were built using "non-traditional construction" using concrete reinforced with steel. In the '80s problems were found with these homes including the corroding of the steel. As a result they're difficult to get mortgages on and so can be bought dirt cheap.

I'm just a bit worried that maybe the steel will cause problems with EMFs? It's in a very rural area, with only a few houses around. The mobile phone reception is really shitty (at least it was a few years ago when I was there) and I doubt there's any broadband.

What gives???

> Can you post what you are eating?

I use ghee, lard, coconut oil. Coconut milk in my coffee.....I can add the fat back in....I was just gaining weight after upping fat, I cant afford to gain any weight. Up 30lbs in the last year. With fluctuations of 10-15 lbs several times.

I am eating fish, shrimp, beef, bacon, chicken. Spinach, broccoli, cauliflower, squash, sweet potato. I have had some apple and pomegranate lately too. Nuts sometimes,

### Inger — 2013-10-24T10:40:45+0300

Make a smoothie or eat them just plain, a little will do because they are very powerful. And of course, seaweeds (but they are wild greens too..lol)

I would not do coconut milk either, do raw coconut oil instead. You can make a delicious coffee with just lots of raw coconut oil, a pinch of salt and then mix it into a nice fatty BPC. You do not need butter for that... or you could add ghee maybe. I always let my coffee cool a bit or put some ice cold water in it before I put the expensive raw coconut oil into it.. I just do not want stuff to get destroyed by heat

I never _ever_ eat chicken..... I would suggest not to do that. Also organic chickens get crap food like wheat and soy, believe me I have seen their feed as we have chickens too. And there is nothing for them to eat in colder months, even if they are pastured. Pastured does not mean they do not get added feed.

Go more raw, it always energizes me....

There is a lot to improve still, which is great, because that means there is much hope you will get way more energy soon  And remember, cutting out dairy does not mean you have to go lower in fat... there are so much nice fat out there to eat still! Do you ever eat lamb? Lambs fat is amazing too... and pretty easy to get, pastured.

I never forget the one member on the Zerocarbforum, his wife had Lyme and very bad. She was so sick all her hair fell out and she tried everything the doctors suggested. Nothing helped. She just got sicker and sicker. Then she started to eat Zero carb with her husband, they eat only pemmican, which is very high fat. Whole family does it together. And she got healthy again , she even got pregnant even ig her docs told her she will never be able to have kids, and have now to amazing and healthy boys, that have never eaten carbs, they just eat pemmican..lol You can see them on youtube if you google his youtube username, chachajoe.

If one would add raw fish and seaweed to that diet it would be the most perfect diet me thinks! Pemmican is also always made of raw, dried meat. Maybe you could try that?

I think... the more ill we are the stricter we have to go with our foods. We might need to go super strict. Super crazy strict! That is what I have had to do myself and I do not regret it

# Thread: Low T And Cortisol
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-t-and-cortisol.8545/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### dheat — 2013-10-24T02:59:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-t-and-cortisol.8545/post-98803>

Can low testosterone in a 50 year old man (251) cause low cortisol in the morning and afternoon?Thanks.

### Huck — 2013-10-24T15:08:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-t-and-cortisol.8545/post-98836>

> [dheat said:](/goto/post?id=98803)
>
> Can low testosterone in a 50 year old man (251) cause low cortisol in the morning and afternoon?Thanks.

Send jansz a pm and ask him to address your question. He will probably post a chart that shows how steroid hormones flow from ldl. If you have low testosterone and low cortisol, you probably also have low pregnenalone, high ldl, low dhea, and on and on. Be prepared to do lots of testing.

When you add 1 hormone, it is a precursor to other hormones, so you have to know what effect adding a hormone has both to that hormone and to hormones it is a precursor to. Again, that's where Jans chart will help.

I never had much success trying to treat a single hormone. I could raise that hormone, but always seemed to have a negative effect on some other hormone. I ended up taking a longer term approach. What has worked best for me is getting light cycles right, eating epi-paleo, ct, reducing emf, grounding, AND getting lots of direct sun on as much of my body as possible and walking.

### dheat — 2013-10-25T04:42:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-t-and-cortisol.8545/post-98973>

Thanks for the info Huck.

# Thread: British cardiologist is getting the message, are you?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/british-cardiologist-is-getting-the-message-are-you.8551/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 21
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2013-10-24T17:31:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/british-cardiologist-is-getting-the-message-are-you.8551/post-98857>

[http://www.latimes.com/science/scie...ed-fat-20131022,0,2193813.story#axzz2iUotmaMg](http://www.latimes.com/science/sciencenow/la-sci-saturated-fat-20131022,0,2193813.story#axzz2iUotmaMg)

The British Medical Journal has issued a clarion call to all who want to ward off heart disease: Forget the statins and bring back the bacon (or at least the full-fat yogurt). Saturated fat is not the widow-maker it's been made out to be, writes British cardiologist Aseem Malhotra in a stinging "Observations" column in the BMJ: The more likely culprits are empty carbs and added sugar.
Virtually all the truths about preventing heart attacks that physicians and patients have held dear for more than a generation are wrong and need to be abandoned, Malhotra writes. He musters a passel of recent research that suggests that the "obsession" with lowering a patients' total cholesterol with statins, and a public health message that has made all sources of saturated fat verboten to the health-conscious, have failed to reduce heart disease.

Indeed, he writes, they have set off market forces that have put people at greater risk. After the Framingham Heart Study showed a correlation between total cholesterol and risk for coronary artery disease in the early 1970s, patients at risk for heart disease were urged to swear off red meat, school lunchrooms shifted to fat-free and low-fat milk, and a food industry eager to please consumers cutting their fat intake rushed to boost the flavor of their new fat-free offerings with added sugar (and, of course, with trans-fats).

The result is a rate of obesity that has "rocketed" upward, writes Malhotra. And, despite a generation of patients taking statins (and enduring their common side effects), the trends in cardiovascular disease have not demonstrably budged.

Malhotra cites a 2009 UCLA study showing that three-quarters of patients admitted to the hospital with acute myocardial infarction do not have high total cholesterol; what they do have, at a rate of 66%, is metabolic syndrome -- a cluster of worrying signs including hypertension, high fasting blood sugar, abdominal obesity, high triglycerides and low HDL ("good" cholesterol).

Meanwhile, research has shown that when people with high LDL cholesterol (the "bad" kind) purge their diet of saturated fats, they lower one kind of LDL (the large, buoyant particles called "Type A" LDL), but not the small, dense particles ("Type B" LDL) that are linked to high carbohydrate intake and are implicated in heart disease.

Recent research has also shown that Mediterranean diets -- admittedly skimpy on red meat but hardly light on saturated fats -- have outpaced both statins and low-fat diets as a means of preventing repeat heart attacks. Other research suggests that the saturated fat in dairy foods may protect against hypertension, inflammation and a host of other dysfunctions increasingly linked to heart attacks.

"It is time to bust the myth of the role of saturated fat in heart disease and wind back the harms of dietary advice that has contributed to obesity," writes Malhotra.

Whether physicians at the front lines of health have gotten the message, a change in thinking is evident, at least, among some of medicine's leaders. But it's not easy to tune out years of what Malhotra calls "the mantra that saturated fat must be removed to reduce the risk of cardiovascular disease."

"When saturated fat got mixed up with the high sugar added to processed food in the second half of the 20th century, it got a bad name," noted UC San Francisco pediatric endocrinologist Robert Lustig. On the question of which is worse -- saturated fat or added sugar, Lustig added, "The American Heart Assn. has weighed in -- the sugar many times over."

Real food includes saturated fat, Lustig said, and real food lives up to the principle that food should confer wellness, not illness. "Instead of lowering serum cholesterol with statins, which is dubious at best, how about serving up some real food?"

\[For the record, 5:20 p.m. Oct. 22: An earlier version of this story misspelled the name of cardiologist Aseem Malhotra.\]

### caroline — 2013-10-25T02:02:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/british-cardiologist-is-getting-the-message-are-you.8551/post-98947>

Dr.K. have your thoughts on full fat cheeses changed at all?

### nicld — 2013-10-25T02:39:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/british-cardiologist-is-getting-the-message-are-you.8551/post-98950>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=98947)
>
> Dr.K. have your thoughts on full fat cheeses changed at all?

Good question Audrey.

People are slowly getting it. We have a poster at work that asked which is a better breakfast, bacon and eggs or a bagel. The winner was bacon and eggs. I keep telling a guy, who I think is younger then me who has high cholesterol that eating fats is better for you then carbs. All he says is his doctors have told him no bacon and eggs. It is just so so sad. He had a teenage daughter that he is raising and probably won't be around all that long for her. Plus he still smokes so until he stops that, everything else is pointless and I remind him that every chance I get.

### nonchalant — 2013-10-25T02:42:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/british-cardiologist-is-getting-the-message-are-you.8551/post-98951>

> [nicld said:](/goto/post?id=98950)
>
> . Plus he still smokes so until he stops that, everything else is pointless and I remind him that every chance I get.

I wonder if vitamin B3 (nicotinic acid) would help smokers quit.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-10-25T03:03:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/british-cardiologist-is-getting-the-message-are-you.8551/post-98952>

cheeses are OK.........but just OK

### caroline — 2013-10-25T03:47:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/british-cardiologist-is-getting-the-message-are-you.8551/post-98960>

I have loved full fat cheese my whole life and always crave them ...which worries me......but - I digest them really well and they make me feel so satisfied - much more so than other proteins or fats. Why would that be?

But cheeses would be at the bottom of the fats/protein list?

### Inger — 2013-10-25T08:12:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/british-cardiologist-is-getting-the-message-are-you.8551/post-98995>

I think I know why cheeses are only ok. They are a very new food (impossible to milk wild animals...). and... when I compare how a MHS / raw cheese makes me feel, the MHS is a clear winner - so clear there is no doubt in my mind the cheese is not optimal. What tastes better? The cheese of course...lol but so do pastries ice creams and pizza.. Audrey you need to test it too

### Hemming — 2013-10-25T12:28:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/british-cardiologist-is-getting-the-message-are-you.8551/post-98999>

Whenever paleolithic people killed an animal I would think they also drank the milk of it. Obviously that's not at all the same as today where cows are being fed all kinds of things, the milk pasteurised and later added substances not fit for human consumption.

### nonchalant — 2013-10-25T16:39:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/british-cardiologist-is-getting-the-message-are-you.8551/post-99010>

> [Hemming said:](/goto/post?id=98999)
>
> Whenever paleolithic people killed an animal I would think they also drank the milk of it. Obviously that's not at all the same as today where cows are being fed all kinds of things, the milk pasteurised and later added substances not fit for human consumption.

Thanks, Hemming. I would think our ancestors drank quite a bit of milk, in season of course. Unless they only killed male animals.

### Hemming — 2013-10-25T16:56:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/british-cardiologist-is-getting-the-message-are-you.8551/post-99015>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=99010)
>
> Thanks, Hemming. I would think our ancestors drank quite a bit of milk, in season of course. Unless they only killed male animals.

I can't tell for all species but cows, buffalos etc. produce milk all year, just in varying quantities, so I don't see it as a seasonal food. I'm not sure when the production of cheese started but the idea of fermenting milk in one way or the other I think goes way back.

### nonchalant — 2013-10-25T17:37:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/british-cardiologist-is-getting-the-message-are-you.8551/post-99017>

> [Hemming said:](/goto/post?id=99015)
>
> I'm not sure when the production of cheese started but the idea of fermenting milk in one way or the other I think goes way back.

I can't tell you how much accidental yogurt I have made with raw milk. You set it outside for the cat and if she doesn't drink it quickly, pretty soon it is solid. Or even sitting on the counter in summer. My chickens liked this yogurt better than the milk it was a couple hours earlier. I think the only ways to stop raw milk from becoming yogurt is to drink it, or refrigerate it.

### Hemming — 2013-10-25T17:44:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/british-cardiologist-is-getting-the-message-are-you.8551/post-99018>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=99017)
>
> I can't tell you how much accidental yogurt I have made with raw milk. You set it outside for the cat and if she doesn't drink it quickly, pretty soon it is solid. Or even sitting on the counter in summer. My chickens liked this yogurt better than the milk it was a couple hours earlier. I think the only ways to stop raw milk from becoming yogurt is to drink it, or refrigerate it.

Hehe, yeah. And I'm sure 'they' realised that too the day when they drink it all straight from the animal.

### Inger — 2013-10-25T18:24:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/british-cardiologist-is-getting-the-message-are-you.8551/post-99021>

> [Hemming said:](/goto/post?id=98999)
>
> Whenever paleolithic people killed an animal I would think they also drank the milk of it. Obviously that's not at all the same as today where cows are being fed all kinds of things, the milk pasteurised and later added substances not fit for human consumption.

Unfortunately it does not work like that Hemming.... I know a guy who hunted a deer that had a calve (he did not ment to, he realized it too late) and he tried to milk the udder from the dead deer but there come no milk. He then cut it open to see if he could get the milk out that way but it was not like you think at all... = impossible. I do not want to get into details because I think it is a bit gross

So in the wild there is no milk to get.

Milk is seasonal for sure. Our neighbor that has goats, must give them additional oats all winter long to get any milk. Hay will not do. In the wild a animal like moose or deer would never have milk all winter long, AFAIK.

So to be honest milk is a new food.. I do not think the American Indians or Eskimos used milk either? Ever seen a Indian miling a buffalo? lol it just is impossible... The Massai do but they have cultivated goats and they live in a different climate too. IDK if their goats give milk year round. Maybe if the goats find enough to eat....

### Hemming — 2013-10-25T18:27:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/british-cardiologist-is-getting-the-message-are-you.8551/post-99022>

Yeah, as I said, it does depend on animal and season.

### Hope — 2014-01-22T01:50:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/british-cardiologist-is-getting-the-message-are-you.8551/post-106556>

Love this article. Just today I was so sad bc I tried to encourage a friend's wife, whose DH at 50 had a quadruple bypass recently and now has issues with arrhythmias etc. and I dared to suggest magnesium, but got shut down. Just so sad. He's on the low salt, low fat diet. Lots of drugs, too...so sad. I am not to mention it again.....breaks my heart.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-01-22T02:31:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/british-cardiologist-is-getting-the-message-are-you.8551/post-106559>

Life is neither static nor unchanging. Change is the only "real evidence" we have in life for life. Evolution is more about adaptivity than adaptability. Those who cannot change their minds cannot change anything. The measure of intelligence is the ability to change. Never fear change. Fear stasis.

### Lahelada — 2014-01-22T02:38:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/british-cardiologist-is-getting-the-message-are-you.8551/post-106563>

Give her time. This is scary times for her. She believes that whitecoat will make it all better and to suggest that she might try something else is like taking out a card at the bottom of a house of cards as far as hope is concerned. At this moment she is a rabbit caught in the headlights.Let her relax and she might come round . I understand your sadness though.

### caroline — 2014-01-22T02:51:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/british-cardiologist-is-getting-the-message-are-you.8551/post-106568>

I have thought long and hard about this .... when my DH was so ill......would I have taken someone else's advice? no matter how well meaning and how heart felt? I think probably not. At the time you are so scared and so worried and so traumatised and there is so many very, very caring Docs and nurses and support staff holding you together and giving their all.

As Lah said - it feels like a house of cards..... and, in our case, we had been supported by all these wonderful and caring professionals for so long ... both me and my DH. of course - now I can ask much better questions and know so much more.

We sometimes forget that is what they were taught - that is what they know .... and that is where Dr. K. was too - only 8 or so years ago.

But - I think magnesium is a given isn't it? I think Dr. K. has told us that some hospitals administer it after surgery?

### Hope — 2014-01-22T03:32:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/british-cardiologist-is-getting-the-message-are-you.8551/post-106576>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=106568)
>
> I have thought long and hard about this .... when my DH was so ill......would I have taken someone else's advice? no matter how well meaning and how heart felt? I think probably not. At the time you are so scared and so worried and so traumatised and there is so many very, very caring Docs and nurses and support staff holding you together and giving their all.
>
> As Lah said - it feels like a house of cards..... and, in our case, we had been supported by all these wonderful and caring professionals for so long ... both me and my DH. of course - now I can ask much better questions and know so much more.
>
> We sometimes forget that is what they were taught - that is what they know .... and that is where Dr. K. was too - only 8 or so years ago.
>
> But - I think magnesium is a given isn't it? I think Dr. K. has told us that some hospitals administer it after surgery?

Thank you guys, and Dr K. Love all you wrote. I know you are right. I guess I have become so passionate.....and so deeply believe this, and see so much suffering, it's just so hard to see...the ongoing FB posts on their anxiety and not knowing......and how the drugs he will get and another surgery to help with the issues.....and here is something they could try. But no openness. I know I have to accept it. I see the same resistance in my DH too. His father just passed after a stroke 3 years ago. 23andme says DH has higher risk for AFib, which led to his dad's stroke. He thinks I am still mostly crazy too, even about blue light, bc it's inconvenient. It too breaks my heart. But at least with DH I have some ability or opportunity for influence. At least with the food, bc he will eat anything I make. he may still get a stroke or whatever....

But yes the magnesium is a given. But.....she just says they checked electrolytes. But I am sure not the right way....ugh. Just so hard, to see the suffering, when there could be another way, maybe. With all we learn here, ugh. Just so sad. It really is about medical deities. Maybe it would be for me too, still, if those MDs just didn't have answers, back in 1998, or couldn't help me, or wanted to silly things like take my gallbladder out or irradiate my thyroid. I knew it wasn't right. I knew nothing about diet and medicine or anything alternative AT ALL in those weeks....but it wasn't right for me. Of course I didn't have all my arteries blocked around my heart, as my friend's husband did. Who knows what I would do if my husband did now.

I guess in the end, it's hard to imagine people walking off a cliff, when there are other paths downhill.....to the valley of health and safety.

Anyway....thanks you guys for chiming in. I AM so thankful for this 'place'. To know I'm not the only one so into all we learn here. It was and is so great too to have events like last night, to hear Dr K and Misty, but even other folks asking questions I too wonder about....

### caroline — 2014-01-22T03:51:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/british-cardiologist-is-getting-the-message-are-you.8551/post-106578>

Hope - your friend just needs your love and support .... it is ingrained in us that our Doctors are trying their best and I really think they are. I have the loveliest Doc now - he truly cares about me and is very interested in all this but when push comes to shove - I need to rely on him and trust his judgement and hopefully I would be alert enough to offer imput.

When it is an acute situation - you just don't have time to research and get other opinions. As you said - you can somewhat influence your DH to some extent ...but in the end the decisions are his to make for himself.....

### Hope — 2014-01-22T16:46:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/british-cardiologist-is-getting-the-message-are-you.8551/post-106625>

I know Audrey....I know. Thanks for the reminder.....


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: This is My Season for this Journey
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-my-season-for-this-journey.8553/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Liveea — 2013-10-24T19:32:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-my-season-for-this-journey.8553/post-98889>

Hello,

I am so pleased to be here. I found Dr. Jack through a post on Facebook. I couldn't stop reading. I was on my way to Minneapolis to get Prolotherapy injections in my back for chronic pain that has persisted for 28 years.

I am 53 years old. I have one son, 26. My chronic pain appear one morning out of the blue, sacrum pain and right shoulder blade area and achy all over. Day before there was no pain. Three things that happened prior to the pain that could answer... I was living in the Black Hills and I had a diving accident that most likely gave me a basal skull fracture with the blood that came out of my ears and the mood problem for several months. (I was young, refused medical care-there was a party that night...I was luck to be able to walk). I had a violent fall on my sacrum. And sometime in that year, I went to the nurse's office at the college with a round rash on my low back that the nurse said was shingles but I know it wasn't. It was just a round ring that was annoying......

Fast forward...searching for medical help for the pain, fatigue, depression, weight loss and weight gain, soo much pain... btw...pain disappeared during the last three months of pregnancy. I was diagnosed with Lyme's disease in 95. Finally got someone to dig deeper as to why I had pain. (I knew nothing about Lyme's then) I test positive 3 times. Had treatment after each test the last was IV rocephin, which made me so violently ill I was in the hospital by the 8th day of treatment.

Side bar....I was concerned because I read that Lyme's crosses the blood placenta barrier. So, we tested my son. He came up positive. No symptoms, but I look back and wonder if his learning disabilities could have been caused by this. He was treated with antibiotics for 10 days regardless of symptoms.

In the hospital, the infectious disease doctor became furious. He screamed at me because a GP doc was treating my Lymes. There had not been a reported case in South Dakota at that point. He was so furious that in fact he refused to go any further with testing or treatment. I was left to deteriorate, die or whatever it was on his brain. So, I had a rheumatologist and a physical medicine doctor fight over the fibro diagnosis or myofascial pain syndrome. I won the Myofascial diagnosis.

I have been to many pain clinics, including Mayo Clinic with no resolve. A lot of drugs along the way....not good. A lot of doctors and medical people dismissing me and telling me it was all in my head. No one would even consider touching the Lyme's tests. I had good insurance and begged.

Still fast forward- car accident- xrays revealed 3 thoracic compression fractures, healed. MRI showed old fx of my sacrum, healed. These were all old and never discovered prior to this....right around 1999.

Weight gain and loss of 45-50lbs about every 3 years. I begged for my thyroid to be tested. I was refused....they said it was fibro and peri-menopause. I kept getting sicker. Gave up a wonderful career due to heal and not being able to deal with the stress of the job, pain and mysterious health issues.

So....finally after jumping doctors...I am getting prolotherapy injections in Mpls and it is helping the over stretch ligaments and tendons...spasms are slowly leaving.
Hypothyroid (now) that swings to hyper we suspect when I loose weight. Hashimoto's is under control and the antibodies are normal now. Severe adrenal fatigue, leaky gut, still have chronic pain, in the weight gain mode.... finally awaiting the Lyme's testing after 20+ years, oh, yes, I have Lichen Schlerosus, and in the 90's gallbladder stopped functioning and had to come out, tonsil's were so infected and I had an abscess burst behind my tonsil, so they came out, I had continued doubling over pain that was the same that I had with the gall bladder symptoms for over a year....tested negative for pancreatitis. I have had chronic unrelenting stress that has added to all of this. Have lost both parents and raised my son solo.

I hope I haven't overwhelmed anyone. I started to see that I was loosing the chronological events. I hope I haven't missed anything.... I hate that my teeth are spreading....doing weird stuff with my tongue...tongue thrusting to bottom teeth and they are spreading..... they say that is neurologic....why? Heaven only knows.

Thanks for reading. I just really felt a connection to everything that Dr. Kruse is talking about. I just have to put it in perspective and act on the protocols. I have ordered some more supplements, working on some changes.

I'm not giving up. I know there is an answer. Thank you again.

Liveea

### caroline — 2013-10-24T23:46:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-my-season-for-this-journey.8553/post-98913>

Hi Liveea and welcome.

My heart just breaks for you........so much for a body to go thru......but you are here now.

Please, please, please start a journal. If you could move this post to the journal section ....Dr. kruse likes journals - he needs to know about you .... context.... to offer any suggestions. A journal will help you with commitment on this journey to optimal health and we can follow along with you and offer support.

The journal section ......top of page ...click on forum and then go 5 categories down and you will see optimal reset and then journals ... just start a new thread and I will watch for you!

Have you read Dr.K's book? it is a great starting point. Please watch Jack's podcasts - they are a quick way to get information.

Please read the easy start guides on the home page and just dive in and then there is a lot to read! it gets easier over time when we are able to start connecting dots!

If you can manage it - become a gold member then you have access to all the webinars and the live Q&A that follows a few days later. You will get the whole library of webinars.

You have a lot of work to do - for sure ......but everyone helps everyone else here .... and Dr. kruse monitors everything.

There are others here who have or had lyme and they will chime in at some point!

Do you have testing that you can post in the Lab section? ...in the forum - go down 7 categories. There are folks here that are great at reading labs.

Don't jump into supplements until you know more.....Dr K believes we should get everything we need from the epi paleo diet and please read about CT ....it is a massive game changer.

I am so glad you found Dr. Kruse ........


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Severe adrenal fatigue, leaky gut, still have chronic pain, in the weight gain mode.... finally awaiting the Lyme's testing after 20+ years, oh, yes, I have Lichen Schlerosus, and in the 90's gallbladder stopped functioning and had to come out, tonsil's were so infected and I had an abscess burst behind my tonsil, so they came out, I had continued doubling over pain that was the same that I had with the gall bladder symptoms for over a year....tested negative for pancreatitis. ### nonchalant — 2013-10-24T23:24:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-a-new-season-for-this-journey.8555/post-98911>

Liveea, welcome!! Glad you are with us.

> In the hospital, the infectious disease doctor became furious. He screamed at me because a GP doc was treating my Lymes. There had not been a reported case in South Dakota at that point. He was so furious that in fact he refused to go any further with testing or treatment. I was left to deteriorate, die or whatever it was on his brain.

Gee, guess they didn't want you to mess up their stats.

I have a relative that had severe pain in the gall bladder area for years. He had it removed, and it seemed to help, but then the pain came back just as bad. He was later diagnosed with ulcerative colitis, and whatever they have him on now is helping. Being in constant pain for years.... is a serious thing for sure.

I assume due to the thyroid issues, I went through menopause at 47. I did have labs to see if I was fully menopaused and checked my testosterone. I am menopaused and my testosterone is normal. Still waiting for my labs on my Lyme's which I am grateful is through IGeneX. We haven't begun to do anything with hormones yet.

It is a tough one. Been pretty sick lately. I believe some of the stuff is detoxing me and I just ache and itch and am irritable and teary eyed. Crazy stuff. Really want to get well.... desperate to get well.

### caroline — 2013-10-25T01:15:54+0300

### Ken23 — 2013-10-25T04:00:14+0300

### caroline — 2013-10-25T04:39:43+0300

### ATL_Paleo — 2013-10-25T04:49:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-just-came-in-26-year-old-male.8557/post-98976>

HS-CRP ideally is below 0.5 .... so your number is okay, but not optimal .... closer to zero, the better.

Free T3 and TSH numbers indicate possible hypothryoidism.

Triglycerides are pretty high ... how clean is your diet? Eating any grains?

Uric acid is high ... once again raises questions about your diet.

am cortisol is good or a bit high ... appears like you have good energy upon waking in the morning

Testosterone looks low ... I assume that is total .... free T is a more meaningful number

Finally .... anytime you post labs, please include the lab ranges .... your labs appear to be from Labcorp ... but as Dr. K says "I hate guessing".

### Ken23 — 2013-10-25T05:13:23+0300

To your point about hypothyroidism- My hands and feet have been ice cold.

Thank you both for responding
Also, not sure this is relevant, but one page of the lab gives diagnosis and said my glucose was consistent with hypoglycemia and that there is evidence of insulin resistance and hyperinsulinemia. The dr. then told me I was pre diabetic. This makes sense as I was having trouble with my blood sugar prior to the diet. With that being said Should I adjust anything as far as adding supplementation? or is this blood sugar problem probably in my rear view mirror?

# Thread: Catalyst Television in Oz debunking saturated far/cholesterol role in heart disease
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/catalyst-television-in-oz-debunking-saturated-far-cholesterol-role-in-heart-disease.8561/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Dextery — 2013-10-25T22:40:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/catalyst-television-in-oz-debunking-saturated-far-cholesterol-role-in-heart-disease.8561/post-99043>

[http://vimeo.com/77730824](http://vimeo.com/77730824) or this link if the vimeo won't play. [http://www.proteinpower.com/drmike/lipid-hypothesis/5257/](http://www.proteinpower.com/drmike/lipid-hypothesis/5257/) or this one [http://www.abc.net.au/catalyst/heartofthematter/](http://www.abc.net.au/catalyst/heartofthematter/)

An excellent Television broadcast to the masses regarding cholesterol & heart disease. Australian Health Foundation is under attack from the grassroots.

This written by Dr Michael Eades who was interviewed along with other physicians.

"Is cholesterol the villain it is made out to be by the medical authorities? Does too much cholesterol cause heart disease? Will it shorten your life? Should you avoid saturated fats to stay free from heart disease? The answers to these questions are not the ones most people would anticipate. The video below provides a different perspective.

About three months ago I got an email from a television producer in Australia informing me she was going to be in the US and would like to interview me. I consented, and the resultant TV show was just shown last night in Oz.

It may be the first time a major television network devoted a serious show to debunking the lipid hypothesis. As readers of the blog are all too aware, most take the opposite approach. Let’s hope this one starts a trend. It would be nice to have an investigative reporter go after a lot of these mainstream lipid guys. As you will see, they look pretty uncomfortable under intensive questioning. Unlike those of us on this side of the debate, they’re not used to being questioned as to the validity of their views."

### caroline — 2013-10-26T00:49:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/catalyst-television-in-oz-debunking-saturated-far-cholesterol-role-in-heart-disease.8561/post-99057>

# Thread: The ultimate key to the Epi Paleo Rx?

### Jack Kruse — 2013-10-26T16:02:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-ultimate-key-to-the-epi-paleo-rx.8565/post-99075>

What makes it superior to paleo template? You do not need any supplements when you really get it right.

Why?

Seafood is a key for getting iodide and iodine to increase myelination. Without proper myelination once loses the ability to take the electrons we split from CSF around our brain that makes an AC current in the CSF to be passed thru our brain and spinal cord's SQUID to electrify neural circuits in our underlying cortex. When the electrons get to the doping mechanism the AC current is rectified to a DC current and that DC current it the current that Robert O Becker found to be regenerating to every animal on this planet.

This statement is explained fully throughout the entire energy and epigenetics blog series. I would suggest you come back to this post and those blogs when you begin to think about the purpose of food for humans.

I'm compiling quotes that sum up the complexity of various biological/hormonal/quantum proceses summed up in a sentence or two. First and foremost, this helps me get the **overall picture**. Then, when I want more detail, I go to a blogpost, read, and look up stuff.

Kinda like being at a cocktail party and someone comes up to you and asks about this crazy epi-shit you're into. You can get the point across without losing them in the details. If you see that glassy-eyed thousand yard stare, you've lost 'em. Hook 'em with the high points, then let them reel themselves in by diving deep into this blog. _(too many seafood metaphors?!)_

The webinars are great for this! When Doc gets on a roll and sums up a hormonal cascade, or what fake light does, or gems like this from the October Quantum Sleep webinar:

"If you don't have iodine, you can't be ketotic, if you can't get ketotic, you can't get into the PPP, you can't make myelin, you can't make the DC current, the more complex your brain is, the more it relies on the DC current, and why is the DC current important?, it allows you to sleep."

_Boom._

When I get confused, I go back and look at quotes like this and things get easier to understand.

Then, it's off to have a cocktail!

BGH, wishing he could actually have a cocktail without going into a coma

# Thread: Mold/biotoxin issues and vacations....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mold-biotoxin-issues-and-vacations.8567/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hope — 2013-10-26T23:33:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mold-biotoxin-issues-and-vacations.8567/post-99096>

So I started studying mold issues, just got the big book and I'm so glad I found it. I don't know why I didn't get it a year ago when I first read the Lyme/Leptin blog but oh well.

But now I am wondering how in the world to go on vacations to new places. I was hoping DH and I could go down to FL or anywhere south for some sun etc. but now I'm not sure, especially after I just realized that our Outer Banks condo that we rented was likely having mold issues. Not only did it smell kinda bad (not too bad only because it was freshly renovated and painted, ugh and I feel like i smelled that more) but I had the worst sleep I have ever had, ever. Like unspeakably bad. I think I now know why. Another place I stayed at for a weekend in Asheville was so moldy, I nearly left the place but was too tired to start to look for anything different in between meetings, ugh.

So I wonder, anyone on here travel with mold issues? How do you figure out where to stay.....ugh. New buildings I guess. I have always been sensitive to smells (in fact I joke I have perfect smell, not perfect vision, as i seem to smell so much other people don't, like even the yucky chemical laundry detergent the neighbors use when their clothes are in the drier).

Would love for someone to respond.....anyone have a good place they can recommend, like even a name of a condo/hotel? There's gotta be some resource...and if not maybe I should start a website. "Lodge here if you have mold issues. Our indoor air is mold-free".

### SeaHorse — 2013-10-27T03:23:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mold-biotoxin-issues-and-vacations.8567/post-99135>

Hope...could you look at some eco- or green travel sites....a lot of them are into natural materials and would be low chemical....but I don't know if that would help you in moldy humid places.

### Hope — 2013-10-27T13:09:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mold-biotoxin-issues-and-vacations.8567/post-99144>

Thanks SeaHorse, yeah I was thinking that too. I did find Hyatt has a pure collection of rooms or something like that. I found eco travel in Costa Rica, but it's extremely pricey from what I have seen so far. That's the hard part. I have NONE of those issues anywhere I have ever stayed in Germany, Austria, Switzerland and even Northern Italy where I used to go. And Luxemburg. And Iceland. Even Poland.

For some reason it's much harder here. I will definitely also ask Dr Shoemaker. HE has a great chapter in his book about DC buildings and how many are WDB.....even the Pentagon may have issues, which made me think of Gretchen. He also shared about clients where the spouse has to 'travel ahead' to find a place that has no mold bf the sick spouse travels down as the potential exposure is so bad. Wow. Also mentioned how mold grows less in certain non-modern buildings....it's all so fascinating to me. It's so amazing. (And I used to hate biology in high school, though that was because we had a drill sergeant for a teacher. The few things I remember are Mendel's laws and the terrible things that happen when you're a drug addict or abort a child. It was good education to not do that.)

Strangely that is what I tried when I was on the Western coast of FL in Cedar Key this year, and all the hotels/condos I went into had a clear musty smell to me, so I wouldn't be going there....ugh.

But I'm not giving up. At all. I will keep looking and finding a place....it may take a long time....but it will be worth it. There is nothing more important than my health and my gut totally tells me that the biotin removal will be a big part for me....

### Shijin13 — 2013-10-27T15:30:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mold-biotoxin-issues-and-vacations.8567/post-99146>

Hope.. I think a lot has to do with construction, as well as proximity to water.

think about the different types of construction the use in Europe - vs what we use here in the states... some of the pre-fab framing already may have issues before new construction goes up.

I never had mold issues in Guam or Okinawa - and I attribute that to the construction materials rebarbed cinder block and cement houses - no carpeting, all ceramic tile floors. every floor had a drain in too - incase of a really bad typhoon.

DC has some serious issues down town - the only major water damage for the pentagon was from putting the fires out on 9/11. but considering there's a good portion that's underground - water is always a possibility... I can smell mold a mile away - As I've lived in a house with stachybacthrus mold...

Not sure how you find out if a location is mold free... but any tips are welcome here....

### Lava — 2013-10-27T16:57:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mold-biotoxin-issues-and-vacations.8567/post-99149>

I have found that most major hotel companies are on top of the mold thing due to lawsuits. The Hilton in Honolulu was a mess and they had to rebuild one building..I tend to stay away from places that are not up to date on mold and bedbugs etc….On Kwajalein the mold was only in the sheetrock buildings..The cement buildings were clean.

Living in Hawaii mold is a real issue and I ask before I stay…also mold mites are a real clue…tiny tiny creatures but they are there if you look hard enough..

### cantweight — 2013-10-27T17:12:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mold-biotoxin-issues-and-vacations.8567/post-99151>

Hope you are connecting so many dots for me....we lived in 2 new construction places in Ashburn one in 1999 and one in 2004 and both were cookie cutter homes thrown up in short periods of time...the whole home exposed to the elements during construction...so much wet wood! My basement in my last house always felt damp/cool/humid. Something i was shocked at when we moved into this older home was the dryness of basement, we all commented on it, realtors and inspectors alike. Mold hadn't crossed my mind but it makes perfect sense another reason we feel better here.

I am like you with the crazy sense of smell....I have often wondered if it was hormonal because during pregnancy was the first time my nose became hyper sensitive and now it always is. I drive my family nuts hunting down smells!

You mentioned the Outer Banks and it really made me think....our best stays there have always been at The Currituck Club. Many places I can remember feeling quite uneasy and not sleeping well, but the times we stayed there were wonderful. The currituck club is sound side and on higher ground than most places, it is somewhat newer construction and the homes have fiber cement siding (something I have been curious about in how it plays with EMF) the homes there never seem damp or musty to me which I had equated with them being newer but it may have more to with construction materials.

Can't wait to hear what Shoemaker says....There isnt a Lyme doc I havent read and he and Klinghardt are my faves hands down! Jemsek is good too I just find him too hyper focused on killing bad things and not strengthening good things, but his neuro understanding is awesome.

### Hope — 2013-10-27T19:27:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mold-biotoxin-issues-and-vacations.8567/post-99157>

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=99146)
>
> Hope.. I think a lot has to do with construction, as well as proximity to water.
>
> think about the different types of construction the use in Europe - vs what we use here in the states... some of the pre-fab framing already may have issues before new construction goes up.
>
> I never had mold issues in Guam or Okinawa - and I attribute that to the construction materials rebarbed cinder block and cement houses - no carpeting, all ceramic tile floors. every floor had a drain in too - incase of a really bad typhoon.
>
> DC has some serious issues down town - the only major water damage for the pentagon was from putting the fires out on 9/11. but considering there's a good portion that's underground - water is always a possibility... I can smell mold a mile away - As I've lived in a house with stachybacthrus mold...
>
> Not sure how you find out if a location is mold free... but any tips are welcome here....

Hi Gretchen, so glad you are OK at the big P there  that is awesome.

As to construction, I have no clue!  Dr Shoemaker talked about an old house in MD he bought having no mold at all, and it was wood. I know in Germany, or Europe, most places I have been, it is not usual for a main home to be built from wood etc, it's all stone/cement etc. Maybe that helps, as well as the air - at least for Germany. No clue why....but it's totally fascinating, not to mention overwhelming.

### Hope — 2013-10-27T19:32:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mold-biotoxin-issues-and-vacations.8567/post-99159>

> [cantweight said:](/goto/post?id=99151)

My basement in my last house always felt damp/cool/humid. Something i was shocked at when we moved into this older home was the dryness of basement, we all commented on it, realtors and inspectors alike. Mold hadn't crossed my mind but it makes perfect sense another reason we feel better here.
>
> I am like you with the crazy sense of smell....I have often wondered if it was hormonal because during pregnancy was the first time my nose became hyper sensitive and now it always is. I drive my family nuts hunting down smells!
>

Many places I can remember feeling quite uneasy and not sleeping well, but the times we stayed there were wonderful. The currituck club is sound side and on higher ground than most places, it is somewhat newer construction and the homes have fiber cement siding (something I have been curious about in how it plays with EMF) the homes there never seem damp or musty to me which I had equated with them being newer but it may have more to with construction materials.
>
> Can't wait to hear what Shoemaker says....There isnt a Lyme doc I havent read and he and Klinghardt are my faves hands down! Jemsek is good too I just find him too hyper focused on killing bad things and not strengthening good things, but his neuro understanding is awesome.

Cristi, that is so great about TCC. I will have to check them out for sure. I love OBX but wouldn't return to the summer rental we had. I read in Dr Shoemaker's book sensitive smell might be a sign of mold issues.....though it can be many other things. I can smell when the neighbor is smoking outside too. It sucks. And I barely have the window open, too, at times.

So so great you now have a basement that does not smell! Friends in the Shenandoah valley, I have two, when entering their houses - OMG it is so much drier and non-smelly than our place here, ugh. That and the non-EMF environment made me want to get out there stat. So we'll see....

I have found a website called The Pure Room, which sounds pretty good.....apparently they check hotels....but that's not everywhere.

I will let you know what I find out.....

### Hope — 2013-10-27T19:34:43+0200

Lava that is fascinating. I didn't know HI had mold issues too, what with all the wind. I'm not sure all hotels are super clean in general, I guess there are varied levels of issues....in Dr S's book many organizations, like schools, even the EPA refuses to say there are mold issues and people have to take matters into their own hands. As we already are....but wow.

I bet HI is amazing though......you have all those great beaches here....

### nicld — 2013-10-27T03:06:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earthing-half-sheet.8572/post-99133>

Does anyone have this and only ground it outside? Did it help? I read that it is not advisable to use this if you need to plug it in to an outlet.

I do not live in a high EMF house and just cannot swing a magnetico pad for our king size bed but would like to do something. With winter coming it is going to get really hard to get outside to ground when there is 2 feet of snow out.

Also anyone use the auto set pad? I spend almost 10 hours a week in my car.

Thanks

Nicole

### Shijin13 — 2013-10-27T15:31:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earthing-half-sheet.8572/post-99147>

I had it and it spiked my hsCRP. from .02 to 4! in under 2 months. I wouldn't use it... you'd be better off putting your mattress in the basement and sleeping on the floor.

I wouldn't use them...

### nicld — 2013-10-27T19:54:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earthing-half-sheet.8572/post-99163>

Thanks for the input Gretchen,

Can't put the mattress in the basement, basement too small (I live in am old farm house). So would getting more magnets help better? I have a 150# pull and a 250# pull now and sleep pretty good but would more help. I want to get some under the hubby too.

Just can't swing a magnetico pad for us, DD needs one first.

### nicld — 2013-10-27T22:10:11+0200
